### Summary:
The Chicago City Council approved several financial transfers and property deals on December 14, 2022. They moved funds within budget committees and allocated additional money to the Department of Family and Support Services for domestic violence and emergency assistance programs. The council also approved a lease for a warehouse facility at 2315 W 27th St and authorized tax levies for special service areas on State Street and in Lakeview East.

The council voted to sell several city-owned properties, including vacant land at 8645 S Yates Blvd to a construction company for office space and property at 2938 E 89th St to Pilsen Wellness Center for a community clinic. Two additional properties were sold to individual buyers under a neighboring property acquisition program, and the city acquired improved property at 518-522 E 47th St.

### Summary:
The City Council introduced seven new proposals in February 2024. These include measures to install protected bicycle lanes across the city, create a plan for adding public bathrooms, extend operating hours for city beaches and pools, and regulate how ride-hailing companies can deactivate drivers. The council also proposed activating the Open Streets program (closing streets to cars) on holidays and busy shopping periods to boost pedestrian traffic.

Additionally, the council introduced proposals on regulated housing accommodations, changes to human rights law enforcement, and a ban on the city contracting with companies involved in immigration enforcement. All of these proposals were referred to relevant committees for further review and discussion.

### Summary:
A citizen petition requests the city update its crosswalk policies to require high visibility markings and stop bars at all pedestrian crossings to improve safety. The Vision Zero Community Advisory Board recommended this change in 2024, and city data shows alarming rates of pedestrian crashes and serious injuries, including at signalized intersections.

Council members questioned a 140-foot bridge project underway at Piestewa Peak, stating it was done without proper public process or apparent Council approval. They noted the preserve requires special protections and alterations must go before Council, and expressed concern that heavy machinery damaged the Lori Piestewa nature trail. Additionally, a resident submitted a petition to eliminate reverse lane traffic on 7th Avenue and 7th Street, which have operated since 1979-1981 and confuse drivers while making it difficult for customers to safely access nearby businesses.

### Summary:
The council approved several land-related items affecting different districts across Phoenix. These included abandoning three easements and rights-of-way in districts 3, 6, and 7, and adopting zoning map amendments in districts 4 and 6. Two rezoning applications were postponed to the February 4, 2026 meeting: one at 35th Avenue and Broadway Road in district 7, and another at 59th Avenue and Lower Buckeye Road also in district 7.

The council also conducted internal business, swearing in municipal court judges and selecting Councilwoman Hodge Washington as the new Vice Mayor. One judge reappointment vote failed. Residents can review the specific land parcels and zoning changes that passed to determine if any affect their neighborhoods or property values.

### Summary:
The city council approved several funding agreements and initiatives. The Department of Public Health will receive over $1 million in revenue from a Medi-Cal Administrative Activities agreement. The Department of the Environment received a $605,620 federal grant from the U.S. Department of Energy to expand an electric bicycle pilot program for delivery workers over three years. The city also authorized $99,000 from the SoMa Community Stabilization Fund to hire consultants to develop a five-year plan addressing community impacts in the SoMa neighborhood.

Additionally, the council approved a 10-year partnership agreement between the San Francisco Public Library and the Friends and Foundation of the San Francisco Public Library to support fundraising and capital projects. The council also passed the first reading of new policies governing surveillance technology at San Francisco International Airport, including rules for ride-share apps, electronic toll readers, and gunshot detection systems.

### Summary:
The board approved a state bill to create a Community Anti-Displacement and Preservation Program that would fund the purchase and rehabilitation of affordable housing. The supervisors also passed several resolutions recognizing community observances, including Child Abuse Prevention Month (April 2026), Pacific Islander communities, Black Maternal Health Week (April 11-17, 2026), and National Crime Victims' Rights Week (April 19-25, 2026).

The board scheduled two public hearings for May 12, 2026, to review sidewalk inspection and repair costs, as well as costs for addressing blighted properties. Additionally, the supervisors supported a federal proposal called the Revitalizing Downtowns and Main Streets Act, which would provide tax credits to convert office and commercial buildings into housing. A resolution recognizing United Airlines' 100th anniversary was referred to committee for further consideration.

### Summary:
The city council approved multiple contracts totaling about $9.7 million for demolition and street work. This includes nearly $664,000 to demolish 43 abandoned residential properties across six districts, $7.7 million for asphalt material to repair streets, about $421,000 for an asphalt paving machine, and $500,000 for asbestos survey services at city facilities. All demolition and street work is fully funded by the city.

The council also considered several other requests: allowing Wayside Missionary Baptist Church to build a safety barrier, approving land encroachments for a company called Giffels Webster, and vacating some streets in Southwest Detroit for Detroit Salt Company. A council member requested a sign inspection at a property on Vandyke and raised questions about a nine percent property and payroll tax withholding. All items were referred to the Public Health and Safety Committee for review.

Stepp, Chairwoman Councilwoman Stepp presented the Transportation Committee report. She said the Committee had lengthy discussion about the transportation needs in the southeastern part of the City. Councilwoman Stepp asked Mr. Cory Steiss, City Traffic Engineer, to present information on the Southeast Gastonia Area Transportation Study (SEGATS). Mr. Cory Steiss explained that the Southeast Gastonia Area Transportation Study (SEGATS) was initiated due to significant growth proposed in the southeast area of the city. He turned the presentation over to Kimley Horn consultant Brady Finklea. Mr. Brady Finklea of Kimley Horne presented a Powerpoint presentation to provide information on the SEGATS study findings which he said evaluates the future of the Union Road and Union New Hope Road Corridor in light of the significant development interest. A copy of the PowerPoint presentation is included in the exhibits. Mr. Finklea said the study is a positive, proactive plan initiated by the City to responsibly manage growth and stated the intent of SEGATS is to serve as a guiding framework for evaluating future developments. Mr. Finklea said the study analyzed current and projected traffic volumes on Union Road and Union New Hope Road corridors. Currently, these are rural two-lane roadways serving schools, neighborhoods, and facilities like the Carolina Speedway with low traffic volumes ranging from 3,500 -115- Page 116 Page 116 REGULAR CITY COUNCIL MEETING – NOVEMBER 6, 2025 to 11,000 vehicles per day. He said typically lanes go from two to four lanes when the traffic volume reaches around 15,000 vehicles per day. There are currently twelve proposed developments totaling 3,700 new residential units plus commercial space. The study projects that by 2055, with all proposed developments, traffic volumes would increase to 17,000 up to 26,000 vehicles per day, well beyond the capacity of the current infrastructure. Mr. Finklea said the study recommends a phased approach for flexibility: Phase 1 (40% development, projected 2030): Intersection improvements at key locations; Phase 2 (70% development, projected 2032): Widening 2.5 miles of roadway between Patrick Road all the way to Nolan Farms; and Phase 3 (100% development, projected 2034): Extending widening another 1.75 miles to the east of Rufus Ratchford Road. He said the recommended road design would include a 110-foot right-of-way, 23- foot median, 11-foot lanes, and multi-use paths on both sides. The estimated cost for road widening is $7,000,000 to $10,000,000 per mile for approximately 4 total miles. Council members expressed concerns about development density and financing for the necessary infrastructure improvements. Mr. Cory Steiss noted that developers have shown mixed feelings about contributing to the costs, as it would impact their profitability. Mayor Franks emphasized that infrastructure improvements should be planned proactively rather than reactively, noting that he has spoken with developers who understand they need to contribute to these costs. Following the presentation, Councilwoman Stepp stated this is not an action item and said staff is requesting to meet in small groups with Council members to have direction on how to move forward with the plan. Councilwoman Stepp said the Committee received a Resurfacing update which was accepted for informational purposes only. Councilwoman Stepp said the Committee received a Transit update which was accepted for informational purposes only. Ms. Randi Gates, Director of Transportation, responded to questions. Councilwoman Stepp requested Mr. Cory Steiss, City Traffic Engineer, to provide information on the Safe Streets for All Grant Traffic Calming Policy. Mr. Steiss explained the Safe Streets for All Grant was advertised in two separate pieces which include a Safety Action Plan and the Traffic Calming Policy. Mr. Steiss turned the presentation over to Jeff Dayton of HDR Engineering. Mr. Jeff Dayton from HDR Engineering presented the Traffic Calming Policy developed as part of the Safe Streets and Roads for All Grant received in June 2023. The policy aims to support the vitality of residential and commercial areas by making streets safer for all users. Mr. Dayton pointed out that this is different and separate from what the City currently has as the existing Speed Hump Policy. Mr. Dayton said the policy outlines a process for both existing neighborhoods and new developments to implement traffic calming measures. For existing neighborhoods, residents can submit applications, which will be evaluated based on scoring criteria. If conditionally approved, applicants will have 30 days to obtain signatures from 75% of residents in the study area. The submittal deadline for -116- Page 117 Page 117 REGULAR CITY COUNCIL MEETING – NOVEMBER 6, 2025 applications would be July 1 each fiscal year, with applicants being notified in August, and implementation beginning in the fall. Mr. Dayton explained the policy includes three funding categories: 100% city-funded, shared-funding, and 100% petitioner-funded, with priority given to the latter. For new developments, developers would be responsible for 100% of the funding for City-required elements. Various traffic calming measures were presented, including vertical deflection (speed tables, raised crosswalks), street width reduction, curve extensions, median islands, roundabouts, on-street parking, routing restrictions, and non-geometric solutions like high visibility and enhanced crosswalks. Council members asked questions about implementation, funding, and how to help residents navigate the petition process. ### Summary:
The city council reviewed a major transportation study for southeast Gastonia, which found that twelve proposed developments could bring 3,700 new homes and significant commercial space to the area. Current roads like Union Road and Union New Hope Road can handle 3,500-11,000 vehicles per day, but the study projects traffic could reach 17,000-26,000 vehicles daily by 2055. The city is proposing a phased plan starting around 2030 to widen about 4 miles of roadway at an estimated cost of $7-10 million per mile, with council members emphasizing that developers should help pay for these infrastructure improvements needed to handle the growth.

The council also approved a new Traffic Calming Policy that will let residents in existing neighborhoods request traffic safety measures like speed tables, narrower streets, and roundabouts. Residents would need to collect signatures from 75% of neighbors to move forward with a project. The city will fund some improvements fully, some partially, and some completely by the petitioner, with priority given to improvements residents pay for themselves. For new developments, builders will be required to fund traffic calming measures the city deems necessary.

### Summary:
The committee reviewed several city contracts and purchases. These include a $134,687 contract with Motorola Solutions to fix the 911 dispatch antenna system, a $481,097 contract extension with Polaris Assessment Systems to handle job processing through June 2017, and three new contracts: a corridor cleaning manager position for $7,920, and two law clerk positions totaling $70,000. The city also approved a $134,687 purchase for 911 emergency dispatch equipment to ensure public safety communications work properly across Detroit.

The committee approved settlements in six lawsuits totaling over $309,500. These include payouts to former city employees for work-related injuries or diseases ($99,000 to Robert Zacharias from Public Works, $96,000 to Charles Nelson from Fire/EMS, $62,500 to Tommy Florence from Public Works, and $30,000 to Kim Wilson from the Water Department), plus settlements to residents Beverly Wakefield ($16,000) and Kimberly Turrentine ($22,000) for injuries sustained in city-related incidents.

### Summary:
The city council voted to override the mayor's veto on two laws about private employer pay practices. The first law (Int. 982-A) requires private employers to report pay data to the city, and the second (Int. 984-A) calls for a study on pay equity in private companies. The council approved both measures with a 40-7 vote, despite the mayor's disapproval in November.

These laws aim to increase transparency around worker compensation and help identify pay discrimination at private employers in the city. The mayor had rejected both measures, but the council had enough votes to pass them anyway.

### Summary:
The council approved multiple service contracts and funding agreements affecting city operations. These included telecommunications licenses for CenturyLink and Level 3 Communications, flood control funding for the Deer Valley Channel project in District 2, and contracts for emergency services like fire incident management and X-ray machine maintenance. The council also authorized easement acquisitions for several drainage and storm drain projects in District 8.

The council approved federal funding to expand family emergency shelter services through the Salvation Army and support affordable housing development at St. Catherine Manor in District 8. A separate proposal for a data center waiver was postponed until December 17. Additionally, the council awarded contracts for HVAC maintenance, mountain preserve trail maintenance, and home inspection services across the city.

### Summary:
The city council approved several zoning and land use changes. These included amendments to city code for zoning maps across multiple districts, approval of historic building grants totaling funds for the Grigsby House in District 8 and seven homes in the Coronado Historic District, and a general plan amendment for the southwest corner of I-17 and Loop 303 in District 1. A rezoning application for the southeast corner of 99th Avenue and Thomas Road was postponed until January 21, 2026.

The council also adopted a new Safe Medical Care in City Parks ordinance citywide, though with a delayed start date of March 30, 2026. During the delay, city staff from Parks and Recreation, Public Health, and Homeless Solutions will meet with healthcare providers to clarify how the rules work and ensure medical professionals can provide services while keeping parks safe for everyone. The ordinance may be revised and brought back to council before it takes effect. Additionally, the council approved changes to roadway crossing and stopping rules.

### Meeting Minutes:
CITY: Chicago DATE: 2023-05-30 BODY: Committee_on_License_and_Consumer_Protection AGENDA ITEM: Attendance AGENDA ITEM: Amendment of Municipal Code Chapter 10-28 by adding new Article X entitled "Outdoor Dining Street" and modifying ancillary Sections 2-25-050 and 9-8-010 ACTION: The Ordinance was Recommended to Pass.

### Summary:
The Committee on License and Consumer Protection voted to recommend a new ordinance that allows outdoor dining on Chicago streets. The change adds a new section to the city's municipal code specifically governing how restaurants can set up and operate outdoor seating areas on public sidewalks and streets.

The ordinance also modifies two related city code sections to support this outdoor dining program. If approved by the full city council, this would give restaurants a new option for expanding their seating capacity and could affect how pedestrians use sidewalks in dining districts.

### Summary:
The committee reviewed several city contracts totaling over $700,000. These included a $32,750 drug testing contract with First Advantage Background Service, four emergency demolition contracts ranging from $15,510 to $298,911 to tear down blighted buildings, and a $4.3 million water shut-off project with Homrich Wrecking.

The committee also discussed multiple ongoing issues brought back for follow-up: senior discounts for trash service, parking enforcement in Brush Park and near schools, drainage fees on city-owned land bank property, addressing blight under viaducts, a broken elevator in city-owned parking, police department grants, an unsecured vacant school building, a new wine makers license application, and medical marijuana enforcement.

### Summary:
The city council approved 16 zoning reclassification changes across various neighborhoods throughout the city. These changes affect properties on streets including Kilpatrick Ave, Polk St, Lake St, Elston Ave, Lincoln Ave, and Northwest Hwy, among others. Alderman Tunney moved to approve each change, and while Alderman Cappleman attempted to reconsider the votes, those motions were unsuccessful.

The specific impacts of these zoning changes—such as whether they allow new types of development, affect property values, or change what can be built in these areas—are not detailed in this summary. Residents in these neighborhoods may want to check with the city planning department to understand how the new zoning classifications apply to their specific areas.

CITY: Chicago DATE: 2022-12-07 BODY: Committee_on_Special_Events,_Cultural_Affairs_and_Recreation AGENDA ITEM: Expenditure of Open Space Impact Fee funds to NeighborSpace for acquisition and development of property at 4131-4149 S Drexel Blvd comprising Kenwood Garden ACTION: The Ordinance was Recommended to Pass. AGENDA ITEM: Expenditure of Open Space Impact Fee funds to NeighborSpace for installation of dedicated water hydrant at Montrose Metra Garden, 4400-4498 N Ravenswood Ave ACTION: The Ordinance was Recommended to Pass. AGENDA ITEM: Expenditure of Open Space Impact Fee funds to NeighborSpace for recreational improvements at Peirce Elementary School, 1423 W Bryn Mawr Ave ACTION: The Ordinance was Recommended to Pass. AGENDA ITEM: Attendance

### Summary:
The committee approved three spending proposals using Open Space Impact Fees—money collected from new developments to fund green space projects. The city will fund NeighborSpace to buy and develop the Kenwood Garden property on South Drexel Boulevard, install a water hydrant at the Montrose Metra Garden on North Ravenswood Avenue, and make recreational improvements at Peirce Elementary School on West Bryn Mawr Avenue.

All three ordinances were recommended to pass, meaning these projects are moving forward to expand and improve parks and recreational spaces across the city.

### Summary:
The city council approved the sale of three vacant city-owned properties to private buyers. A vacant lot at 20133 Van Dyke will be sold for $3,746 to be used as parking for an adjacent barbershop. A property at 7515 Melrose will be sold for $2,060 to someone planning to rehabilitate it into a single-family home—the buyer must secure the building within 6 months and obtain occupancy approval within 30 months. A third property at 4724/4728 Conner will be sold for $4,500 to be converted into a parking lot for an auto repair business next door.

All three sales are permitted under the current zoning for each location. The council also approved a five-year capital improvement plan recommendation from the Planning Director for fiscal years 2017-18 through 2021-22.

### Summary:
A developer wants to annex and rezone about 83 acres west of Davis Park Road to build a 222-unit single-family residential subdivision. The property is currently in Gaston County with some existing homes and mostly wooded land. The project includes a pocket park, playground, walking trails, and connection to a future public greenway along Crowders Creek. The Planning Commission unanimously recommended approval, and city staff also supports it.

Council members raised concerns about traffic and stormwater management in the area. The developer must widen local roads to at least 20 feet (as required by state standards), complete a traffic impact analysis, install stormwater management systems, and implement any traffic mitigation measures. The council has not yet voted on final approval.

### Summary:
The Denver Finance and Business Committee postponed votes on several janitorial service contracts worth nearly $26 million combined that would cover city buildings through April 2029. These contracts with three different companies will be presented at a later date for final approval.

The committee also approved the Five Points Business Improvement District to continue operating for another ten years, and set a public hearing for the Welton Corridor Urban Redevelopment Plan. A separate real estate purchase of a Capitol Hill building at 251 East 12th Avenue for $4.5 million was postponed for future consideration.

### Summary:
The Board of Supervisors referred five landmark designation proposals to committee for review. These would protect historic buildings from demolition or major changes: the Power House at 1526 Masonic Avenue, Second Church of Christ Scientist at 651 Dolores Street, Shaughnessy House at 394 Fair Oaks Street, St. Aidan's Church at 601 Belvedere Street, and Tietz/Beneke House at 657 Chenery Street.

The board also advanced several zoning and building code changes for committee review. The city updated its Fire Code to adopt 2025 California standards effective January 1, 2026. A separate proposal would allow the One Oak Street building project to increase its podium height from 120 feet to 140 feet. Additionally, a new ordinance would require contractors hired for city street cleaning services to pay prevailing wages.

### Summary:
The Board of Supervisors voted to delay decisions on several zoning and building projects to April 7, 2026. These include an appeal about adding a dwelling unit to a four-unit building in Telegraph Hill-North Beach, and two related motions about conditional use authorization for a project at 524-526 Vallejo Street and 4-4A San Antonio Place. One resident spoke against postponing the first appeal, but the board voted unanimously to continue all three items to a later date.

The board also approved three lawsuit settlements on first reading. The city will pay $55,000 to settle an employment dispute with Kisha Henderson, $800,000 for a wrongful death claim filed by Renee Owens related to a sidewalk incident, and $500,000 to settle a lawsuit involving multiple members of the Pham family. All three settlements passed with unanimous support from the supervisors.

### Summary:
The city council approved three grants totaling over $381,000. The first two grants—$50,000 and $75,000 from the Massachusetts Office of Travel & Tourism—will fund senior programs in Roxbury and Dorchester. The third grant of $256,500 from Mass Save will pay for outreach efforts to help low-income neighborhoods participate in energy efficiency programs.

The council also scheduled several hearings to examine city issues including healthcare access (telehealth and primary care doctors), residential property tax assessment practices, transportation infrastructure and funding, nightlife industry safety, and police overtime costs. These hearings will be reviewed by various committees before any action is taken.

### Summary:
The city approved a $118.8 million increase to its contract with HomeBridge, Inc. for in-home supportive services for seniors and people with disabilities. The agreement now totals $158.4 million and runs through June 2029, providing care services to residents who need help living independently at home.

The city also approved two sidewalk repair programs managed by Public Works. One covers general sidewalk and curb repairs citywide, while the other targets repairs in blighted areas. Additionally, the Department of Public Health amended its lease for the Chinatown Public Health Center location, extending it and increasing the budget for building improvements by $500,000 to $800,000 total.

Ms. Anderson closed by expressing gratitude as a Phoenix native and reaffirmed her commitment to helping make Phoenix a diverse, welcoming, and accessible community for all. \par \par Mayor Gallego thanked staff for the presentation and Councilwoman Stark for spearheading the workgroup and its efforts. \par \par Councilwoman Stark stated she became more aware of accessibility needs as she has aged, noting she recently turned 70. She acknowledged many people have loved ones with unique needs and thanked the committee for reflecting those values. She expressed pride in serving on the committee and gratitude for the group\rquote s work. Councilwoman Stark thanked Ms. Erickson for years of collaboration and said the work is deeply important to her. \par \par Vice Mayor O\rquote Brien congratulated Councilwoman Stark and the committee, thanking them for their dedication. She mentioned touring First Place and praised its supportive housing model, expressing interest in expanding similar projects Citywide. She also thanked Inger for her outstanding career and wished her well in retirement. \par \par Councilwoman Hodge Washington thanked the committee for its thoughtful and intentional work, noting able\u8209?bodied individuals often take accessibility for granted and these efforts will significantly benefit Phoenix residents. She expressed appreciation for the team\rquote s passion, commended Councilwoman Stark and Inger for their leadership, and thanked the Mayor for prioritizing accessibility, stating true inclusion ensures all residents can fully participate in City life. \par \par Councilwoman Hernandez expressed thanks to the Mayor, staff and the committee. She emphasized the City\rquote s investments in accessibility and community resources are deeply meaningful for families whose loved ones may not be able to live independently and added Phoenix\rquote s leadership in accessibility demonstrates the City\rquote s commitment to being welcoming and inclusive, ensuring all residents have the resources needed to thrive. Councilwoman Hernandez asked Ms. Erickson to explain how staff could identify future goals. \par \par Ms. Erickson stated the work is transitioning to the MCDI, which will continue pursuing completed and unfinished goals with City staff to maintain progress. She noted the subgroup had only one year but achieved significant results, and the commission will expand work in partnership with Equal Opportunity and other departments. \par \par Councilwoman Hernandez asked if Ms. Erickson to provide a list of current projects or initiatives the commission will work on. \par \par Ms. Erickson replied yes, the list will be provided. \par \par Councilwoman Hernandez instructed the list to include specific areas of the City. \par \par Councilwoman Pastor thanked Ms. Erickson and shared her appreciation for hearing loop technology has been implemented Citywide. She emphasized the importance of accessible traffic signals and expanded implementation along 7th Street and Thomas to better connect accessible housing and services. She also expressed pride in the accessibility progress made and thanked the committee for their efforts. \par \par Judith Clark expressed her appreciation for the resources in Phoenix and shared her experience of not receiving access to the Ride Choice Program due to her receiving services from the Dial-a-Ride Program. Ms. Clark requested an explanation on what is being done about the lack of service. \par \par Mayor Gallego noted the first feature length film filmed in the City with our tax credit after the State passed the tax credit was Unstoppable, the story of Anthony Robles, the wrestler at Arizona State University who overcame so many challenges, including being born without one leg to be a national champion. \par \par } AGENDA ITEM: 2024-25 Year-End General Fund Budget Results and Adoption of the 2026-27 Budget Calendar - Citywide ACTION: A motion was made that this item be adopted. He introduced Deputy City Manager Amber Williamson to present the general fund budget results. \par \par Ms. Williamson presented an overview of the FY 2024-25 general fund results and highlighted the City\rquote s strong year-end position despite national and State economic challenges. She acknowledged the high accuracy of the Budget and Research Department, noting revenue and expenditure variances were all under 1 percent, which she described as exceptional performance given the scale of the City\rquote s multi-billion dollar budget. \par \par Ms. Williamson reported general fund resources, including beginning fund balance, revenues, and inter-fund transfers, ended the fiscal year slightly over 15.7 million dollars, a variance of 0.7 percent. ### Summary:
The council celebrated completion of a year-long accessibility initiative focused on making Phoenix more inclusive for residents with disabilities. The work, which included ADA improvements at city parks and expansion of hearing loop technology, will now be handed off to the city's Multicultural Development Initiative to continue pursuing completed and unfinished goals in partnership with other city departments. Council members praised the effort as a reflection of Phoenix's commitment to ensuring all residents can fully participate in city life.

The city approved the 2026-27 budget calendar after finishing the 2024-25 fiscal year in strong financial condition. City officials reported revenues came in nearly exactly as projected, with less than 1 percent variance on a multi-billion dollar budget—described as exceptional performance. The general fund ended with slightly over $15.7 million in resources, demonstrating careful budget management despite economic challenges.

### Summary:
The Seattle Housing, Arts, and Civil Rights Committee met on March 11, 2026, to confirm several appointments. The committee recommended City Council confirm Carlin Llorente to the Burke-Gilman Place Public Development Authority through the end of 2026, and reappoint eight members to the Seattle Arts Commission: Avery Barnes, Yoon Kang-O'Higgins, Yolanda L. Spencer, Linda Chavez-Lowry, Diana Garcia, Ricky Graboski, and Megan Kiskaddon, all for terms through December 31, 2027.

The committee also heard an informational presentation from the Seattle Department of Construction and Inspections about rental housing programs. The meeting ran from 2:05 p.m. to 3:42 p.m., and residents were invited to submit public comment in advance or during the meeting either remotely or in person at City Hall.

### Summary:
The City Council advanced several resolutions calling for action on public safety and health issues. These include pushing the federal government to regulate e-bike battery safety, opposing National Grid's plan to install gas vaporizers at Newtown Creek, requiring SUNY and CUNY to stock emergency contraception in vending machines, and supporting state legislation to lower speed limits in the city and establish victims' rights protections.

The Council also introduced new local laws requiring apartment building owners to post notices about electric space heater safety, launching an annual education campaign against antisemitism, expanding paid sick leave protections for more workers, and increasing outreach about immigration assistance fraud. All items were referred to relevant committees for further review.

### Summary:
The committee approved a request from Mt. Vernon Missionary Baptist Church to host their 30th Annual May Day Parade and Celebration on May 20, 2017. The event will run from 10 a.m. to 3 p.m., stretching from Fenkell and Burt Road to Eliza Howell Park, with temporary street closures on Fenkell. All city departments recommended approval.

The committee also corrected a lease agreement with People's Community Services of Metropolitan Detroit for the Delray Recreation Center. The contract end date was extended from December 31, 2016 to December 31, 2036, ensuring the organization continues managing utility payments at the facility. Additionally, the committee discussed parking issues at Comstock Park with the Recreation Department and Detroit Police 12th Precinct.

### Summary:
The committee reviewed several city contracts and service agreements. These included an IT support manager contract for $71,600, a candidate processing contract worth $481,097 to support restructuring of the city finance office, and a $62,955 contract to purchase nine utility carts for park maintenance. The committee also discussed a proposed change to Detroit's fireworks regulations to align with state law by removing local rules on the transport and sale of fireworks and business licensing for fireworks sellers.

Additionally, the committee corrected details on several existing contracts, including adjusting the end date of a $750,000 park repairs contract from 2017 to 2018 and updating the hourly wage for an election department IT and advertising worker from $17.31 to $19.52 per hour. The committee also considered items related to tax collection on foreclosed properties and a prevailing wage ordinance.

MINUTES: {\rtf1\ansi\ansicpg1252\deff0\nouicompat\deflang1033{\fonttbl{\f0\fnil\fcharset0 Arial;}} {\colortbl ;\red0\green0\blue0;} {\*\generator Riched20 10.0.22621}\viewkind4\uc1 \pard\cf1\fs28 The Phoenix City Council convened in Policy Session on Tuesday, March 29, 2025, at 2:30 p.m. in the Council Chambers.\par } MINUTES: {\rtf1\ansi\ansicpg1252\deff0\nouicompat\deflang1033{\fonttbl{\f0\fnil\fcharset0 Arial;}} {\colortbl ;\red0\green0\blue0;} {\*\generator Riched20 10.0.22621}\viewkind4\uc1 \pard\cf1\fs28 Mayor Gallego recognized the Parks and Recreation Department for providing inclusive recreation opportunities through adaptive programs, strategic partnerships, and facility improvements. \par \par Deputy City Manager John Chan emphasized the City\rquote s is committed to ensuring all residents, regardless of ability, have access to meaningful, high-quality, recreational amenities and opportunities.\par \par Parks and Recreation Director Cynthia Aguilar stated the City of Phoenix Parks and Recreation System has 188 flat land parks, 40,000 acres of preserve and desert parks, 33 recreation centers, 29 aquatic facilities, and 8 public golf courses. Ms. Aguilar stated there are various adaptive recreation programs and partnerships which help create an adaptive environment for all. \par \par Mr. Whitfield recognized one of the longest running and most impactful adaptive program is the Partners Program which is in partnership with the Arizona Department of Economic Security (DES) Division of Developmental Disabilities. This Program is provided to adults and focused on life skills, social-emotional growth and cultural enrichment. Mr. Whitfiled noted a key component of the Program is the partnership with Special Olympics which allows participants to train in sports like basketball and cheerleading. Further, he stated the Department added two adaptive recreation coordinators who coordinate daily programming, special events, manage community and school outreach, as well as ensure compliance with the Arizona DES Program requirements such as documentation. \par \par Ms. Barrett detailed various year-round inclusive programs for all ages and disabilities which include enrichment, open gym, adaptive sports, and holiday events. She noted the City partners with Ability360 to provide adaptive on-course play at Encanto 9 and 18 Golf Courses from November to May. Ms. Barrett explained that the therapeutic benefits for individuals with physical limitations include stimulating metabolism, stretching the muscles, reducing spasticity, and enhancing joint mobility. She spoke about how the Adaptive Golf Team helps coordinate the Special Olympics Arizona Regional Golf Championship, as well as the Arizona Adaptive Open Championship in partnership with the Arizona Golf Foundation. Ms. Barrett noted the diverse operation of adaptive recreation is also provided at the Telephone Pioneer Park which has programming for art development, recreational clinics, and inclusive celebrations and events which includes a total of 200 classes offered annually. She shared that inSummer 2025, Ability360 would be expanding its offerings to include an inclusive aquatics program at Telephone Pioneer Pool which had been closed since 2020. Further, she shared the City also offers inclusive outdoor experiences beyond the City\rquote s limits through a partnership with the Harden Education and Recreation Therapy Foundation at Camp Colley, which blends recreation with personal development in a natural setting. \par \par Mr. Whitfield emphasized the City\rquote s commitment to building and maintaining accessible and inclusive parks for all residents. He stated the Parks and Recreation Department allocates funding every year specific for Americans with Disabilities Act (ADA) improvements, as well as use Phoenix Parks and Preserve funding to address ADA issues when they arise. He recognized Mountain View Park which has a new ADA accessible parking lot and the Dreamy Draw Park improvement which includes accessible trails. Mr. Whitfield said as a part of the General Obligation Program, the Parks Department was awarded $2.2 million towards renovating the recreation center at Telephone Pioneer Park which included reconfiguring doors and entrances, updating restrooms to comply with ADA standards, and a new fire suppression system. He also noted there was a new sensory room at Cesar Chavez Community Center. Mr. Whitfield also highlighted the new ADA playground at Encanto Park which opened in May 2024 in partnership with the Kiwanis Club of Phoenix. He said the updated playground features include swings, slides, spinners, sensory elements, and social spaces that encourage inclusive interaction for all. A key feature is the communications board which is design to support nonverbal or speech challenged children, helping them engage with peers and caregivers. \par \par Ms. Aguilar recognized the members of the Adaptive Recreation Team which contribute to the success of the Programs mentioned. \par \par Mayor Gallego recognized the team, and noted the City of Phoenix was the first to have an accessible park. \par \par Chelsea Harden commented on the importance of Camp Colley for neurodiverse and neurotypical youth. \par \par Mayor Gallego asked if registration was still open. \par \par Ms. Harden confirmed. \par \par Gus Lazear expressed appreciation for the City\rquote s partnership

### Summary:
The Phoenix City Council recognized the Parks and Recreation Department's work creating inclusive recreation opportunities for residents with disabilities. The city operates 188 parks, 33 recreation centers, and 29 aquatic facilities, many with adaptive programs. Key initiatives include the Partners Program (partnering with the Arizona Department of Economic Security to serve adults with developmental disabilities), adaptive sports programs through Special Olympics, adaptive golf courses, and inclusive aquatics programs launching in Summer 2025 at Telephone Pioneer Pool after being closed since 2020.

The city is investing in accessibility improvements across parks and facilities. Recent projects funded by a $2.2 million General Obligation Program grant include renovating Telephone Pioneer Park's recreation center to meet disability access standards, adding a new ADA-accessible playground at Encanto Park (opened May 2024 with swings, sensory elements, and communication boards for nonverbal children), accessible parking and trails at Mountain View Park and Dreamy Draw Park, and a new sensory room at Cesar Chavez Community Center. The city also partners with Ability360 and other organizations to provide adaptive programming and outdoor recreation experiences for all ages and abilities.

### Summary:
The Budget, Finance and Audit Committee reviewed several contracts and funding requests. The city approved a $374,930 contract with Magnet Consulting for hiring and screening services through June 2017, a $57,000 contract with Donna Brown to manage the tax database system, and a $150,000 contract with Value Trends Inc. to reappraise city properties. The committee also authorized the city to apply for a $2 million grant from the Michigan Department of Treasury for financially distressed cities, with no local match required.

The committee discussed a proposed five-year capital plan for 2017-2022 and considered amending the Special Assessment Districts rule to require 70% homeowner approval before establishing a special assessment district. The committee also reviewed matters related to economic strategy, vendor compensation, changes to the Chief Financial Officer's office, and prevailing wage enforcement, all of which were carried over from previous meetings.

### Summary:
Boston City Council approved two major school improvement projects totaling $21.3 million. The first project allocates $20.2 million for window and door replacements at Adams Elementary School, while the second dedicates $1.1 million for heat pump conversions and roof replacements at Margarita Muñiz Academy, Mildred Avenue K-8 School, and Orchard Gardens School. The city expects to receive grants from the state's School Building Authority to help cover these costs.

The council also accepted two smaller grants: $20,000 from the state for a food working group that will help Boston Public Schools and other institutions coordinate food purchasing and recovery efforts, and $13,735 for workforce development programs. Additionally, the council approved a special pension benefit for retired firefighter Leo J. Bracken.

### Summary:
The City Council approved three related land use applications for a major development project in Brooklyn's Williamsburg area (near Williams Place, Herkimer Street, and Atlantic Avenue). The approvals allow for a mixed-use development that includes new public open space, commercial retail space, and mixed-use buildings. The projects received modifications and were referred to the City Planning Commission for final approval.

The developments will modify zoning rules to allow larger retail spaces and flexibility in how buildings are positioned and sized across the multiple properties involved. All three applications were approved on December 18, 2025, and will now move forward to the City Planning Commission for final review.

### Summary:
The Seattle City Council met on March 3, 2026, and passed two resolutions. One resolution addresses law enforcement conduct, reaffirming city values around professional policing and directing the Seattle Police Department to document and report violations of professionalism standards. The council also condemned recent actions by federal law enforcement agents.

The council also adopted a resolution about developing a regional transportation hub near Westlake Park. The resolution sets a vision for how this hub will work with nearby neighborhoods and establishes guiding principles for public projects planned in that area, focusing on functionality, safety, and compatibility with the surrounding community.

### Summary:
The Board of Supervisors postponed decisions on a proposed development project at 524-526 Vallejo Street and 4-4A San Antonio Place until March 10, 2026. The Planning Commission had denied permission for the project, and the Board was considering whether to override that decision and approve it, but chose to delay the vote.

The Board passed a new rule allowing movie theaters in the Upper Fillmore neighborhood that serve food to offer wine and beer sales without being limited by the district's usual size restrictions for bars. Additionally, the Board set a hearing date of March 17, 2026 to consider closing portions of Christmas Tree Point Road and Twin Peaks Boulevard so those streets can be converted to park and recreation space for the Twin Peaks Promenade Project.

### Summary:
Council voted to approve several transportation and infrastructure projects. The city will dedicate new public streets including West 38th Avenue, West 10th Avenue, and West 11th Avenue as part of the city street system. The council also approved a $2.76 million grant agreement with the Colorado Department of Transportation to rebuild the intersection of Alameda and Dayton Streets to improve safety and reduce crashes. Additionally, the city approved a $964,000 grant with the Regional Transportation District to study transportation alternatives for the Welton Street corridor.

The council also approved a permit allowing Public Service Company of Colorado to install and maintain utility infrastructure at the 39th Avenue Greenway near North York Street and East 39th Avenue at no cost to the city. The permit allows 90 days for installation followed by ongoing access and maintenance rights.

Washington questioned how the new RMS would help improve the public's access to disaggregated data on stops, arrests and citations.\par \par Mr. Orender invited Ms. Wolf to talk about the new RMS.\par \par Police Chief of Staff Jody Wolf responded the Police Department captured a multitude of data fields and metrics associated with the activities executed by the department. She said the intent was to improve the data collection procedures so they were consistent both in what was collected and how it was collected so that it was always collected. She conveyed once data was collected the raw data associated with all activities from an enforcement perspective were placed on the open data source. She mentioned there was also the police transparency page which provided information related to specific metrics that were of interest.\par \par Councilwoman Hodge Washington requested staff explain some of the proposed audit function to ensure there was no disproportionate impact on certain communities.\par \par Mr. Orender remarked that was missed in the past, but the Police Department would be doing audits and compliance checks to make sure the department was doing what it said it was doing. He emphasized the department recognized it would not arrest its way out of low level crime or crime in general. He expressed his frustration as a young police officer when he came into contact with someone who had been arrested multiple times so it did not solve the problem.\par \par Councilwoman Pastor asked if OHS staff was present.\par \par Assistant City Manager Inger Erickson advised OHS staff was not present as they had to attend another meeting.\par \par Councilwoman Hodge Washington asked how the Community Assistance Program handled quality of life calls.\par \par Program Manager Dolores Ernst responded when the Community Assistance Program was contacted to respond to a call a behavioral health unit was sent, noting the unit was staffed with a crisis intervention specialist and a peer support specialist from a community partner. She conveyed their job was to go out and assess the individual, determine what their needs were and help them get either connected or reconnected with services.\par \par Councilwoman Hodge Washington recalled one of the goals Council set for the program last year was to increase staff to provide access 24/7 in some areas and wanted to know where the program stood regarding that goal.\par \par Ms. Ernst replied in March 2025 the program met the requirement of having 24/7 coverage with a crisis intervention supervisor in the alarm room to accept transferred calls from Police Department communications. She added the program worked with community partners to connect individuals who were not involved in these services that were affected by mental health or substance use and unable to make decisions about accessing quality services.\par \par Ms. Erickson added OHS always lead with services and had a good track record of working with different departments to find the necessary resources when staff encountered homeless individuals.\par \par Councilwoman Hodge Washington recalled there was testimony about data collection regarding interactions with the public that lead to interactions with ICE. She indicated about four percent of the bookings lead to immigration holds, but staff could not verify what that meant for those individuals after that point. ### Summary:
The Police Department presented its new records management system (RMS) aimed at improving data collection on stops, arrests, and citations. The department will make raw enforcement data available to the public through an open data portal and a police transparency page. To address concerns about unequal treatment, the Police Department will conduct audits and compliance checks at the precinct level and through its Integrity Bureau to ensure the department is not disproportionately impacting certain communities.

Council members also discussed how the city responds to quality-of-life calls involving homelessness and behavioral health issues. The Community Assistance Program has expanded to provide 24/7 coverage with behavioral health units staffed by crisis intervention and peer support specialists who connect people to services. The program currently operates nine units with nearly round-the-clock availability (with some gaps on Friday and Saturday). Additionally, the city is investigating data about how many police interactions lead to immigration holds through the county jail system, though officials noted they cannot currently track what happens to individuals once they're in custody.

### Summary:
Denver City Council approved four federal housing grants totaling approximately $7 million. The grants include $2.4 million for general housing programs citywide, $580,000 for emergency shelter and homelessness prevention, and $4 million to support housing for low-income people living with HIV/AIDS. Council also approved changes to an affordable housing preservation project at 1625 and 1675 S Birch Street in Council District 6, modifying how the project will be financed without changing the total agreement amount.

The council also honored Pamela Walshe with a proclamation for her work addressing food insecurity in southeast Denver. The meeting included standard procedural items like the pledge of allegiance and approval of previous meeting minutes.

### Summary:
The Zoning Committee reviewed dozens of zoning reclassification requests across Chicago neighborhoods and voted to recommend most of them for passage. These changes allow property owners to change how their land can be used—for example, converting areas zoned for one type of development to another. Several proposals were recommended to pass, affecting properties on Columbus Avenue, Cicero Avenue, Western Avenue, and multiple other locations throughout the city.

The committee also discussed amending rules for Planned Development 1092 and reviewed parking and loading zone changes on West Congress Parkway. Most of the zoning changes reviewed moved forward with committee approval, though the full meeting minutes don't indicate which specific proposals were rejected or require further discussion before the full city council votes on them.

### Summary:
The committee reviewed the status of casino development funds, confirming that Detroit's three casinos (MGM Grand, Motor City, and Greektown) each contributed $10 million to the city for business development. They also examined a report on the Detroit VEBA retirement fund, which revealed that bonds issued to cover retiree benefits were worth only 45% of their promised value, creating a shortfall in what retirees were promised.

The committee approved a $374,930 contract with Magnet Consulting to handle job interview screening and coordination through June 2017, and referred two applications for Neighborhood Enterprise Zone certificates (at 1215 Griswold and Michigan Avenue/Book Cadillac) to move forward for approval. The committee also reviewed the city's risk management exposure, which includes $152.6 million in potential liabilities from worker injuries, legal claims, and accidents.

### Summary:
The Seattle Parks and City Light Committee discussed an ordinance authorizing the city to execute settlement and licensing agreements for relicensing the Skagit River Hydroelectric Project with the Federal Energy Regulatory Commission. The discussion included a 45-minute briefing from City Light leadership and legal staff, followed by a 30-minute panel where representatives from three local tribes—the Sauk-Suiattle Indian Tribe, Swinomish Indian Tribal Community, and Upper Skagit Indian Tribe—shared their perspectives on the project.

No final vote or decision was recorded in these meeting notes. The meeting focused on gathering information and hearing from affected tribes before any council action is taken on the relicensing agreement.

The motion carried by the following voice vote: MINUTES: {\rtf1\ansi\ansicpg1252\deff0\deflang1033{\fonttbl{\f0\fnil\fcharset0 Arial;}} {\colortbl ;\red0\green0\blue0;} \viewkind4\uc1\pard\cf1\b\fs28 Discussion\par \b0 A motion was made and seconded to continue this item to the July 2, 2025 Formal meeting.\par \par Vice Mayor O'Brien remarked that Council just voted for a tax increase on residents in part to help cover the City's $27 million of on-going costs to provide services and shelters to the homeless population, noting Keys to Change was almost one-third of that continuing annual cost. She recalled last year when she fought for a seat on Central Arizona Shelter Service's (CASS) board and was seeking the same on this item. She said staff had approached her with concerns about Keys to Change and their financial issues due to lack of continuous transparency and operating deficits. She inquired if Keys to Change had expressed concerns to the City related to a financial deficit.\par \par Assistant City Manager Inger Erickson replied she remembered last year staff had concerns with the organization regarding funds.\par \par Homeless Services Special Projects Administrator Annette Medina requested Vice Mayor O'Brien repeat the question.\par \par Vice Mayor O'Brien asked if there were concerns related to Keys to Change's financial deficit and whether the organization shared that information with the City.\par \par Ms. Medina responded there were concerns with the overall deficit of $5 million.\par \par

### Summary:
The city council approved its 2025-26 budget, which includes about $27 million in ongoing costs for homeless services and shelters. The council also approved amendments to the 2024-25 budget. Vice Mayor O'Brien noted that residents are facing a tax increase partly to cover these expenses, and she raised concerns about Keys to Change, a homeless services organization that receives nearly one-third of the city's homelessness funding. Staff indicated the organization has a $5 million deficit and has lacked transparency in its finances.

Councilwoman Hernandez voted against the budget, arguing it doesn't adequately address root causes of homelessness and that the city invests too heavily in police instead of alternatives. A community member also spoke against the budget, pointing out the city allocated $66 million to the Police Department while claiming it lacked funds for youth programs. A decision on expanding the contract with Keys to Change was postponed until early July.

### Summary:
The council held public hearings on three separate applications for Obsolete Property Rehabilitation Certificates. These certificates allow property owners to get tax breaks when they renovate abandoned or run-down buildings. The three projects are: Danish Brotherhood Apartments at 1775 West Forest, a property at 6402 Woodward Avenue, and another at 6408 Woodward Avenue. All three are being considered under state law that encourages redevelopment of neglected properties.

The agenda also included items from the Office of Contracting and Procurement regarding city contracts and a meeting of the Historic Designation Advisory Board, though no details about votes or decisions on these items are provided in the text.

### Summary:
The Denver Health and Safety Committee approved a $150,000 contract with Axon Enterprise to install 50 automatic license plate recognition cameras across the city. This surveillance system will be used for public safety purposes and runs through March 2027.

The committee also approved three separate contracts: adding $250,000 to the forensic pathology services contract with 5280 Forensic Pathology (bringing it to $633,000 total), adding $1,000,000 to Dr. Meredith Frank's forensic pathology contract (bringing it to $1,383,000 total), and modifying the Goodwill of Colorado contract to allow for employee retention bonuses and PTO payouts while continuing case management services for residents receiving temporary assistance.

### Summary:
The council approved seven major development projects using tax increment financing (TIF), a funding tool that directs future tax revenue toward redevelopment. Projects include affordable housing on Chicago Avenue, a performing arts theater on Cottage Grove Avenue, the Aspire Center workforce training facility on West Madison Street, office and community space on North Avenue, health center and retail renovation of the former Goldblatt buildings on Ashland Avenue, and renovation of the Congress Theater on Milwaukee Avenue into an entertainment venue with residential units and retail space.

The council also approved park improvements at Winnemac Park including renovated sports fields, additional lighting, and paved pathways. In other business, the council made a minor correction to the City Council journal and passed amendments regarding criminal history requirements in city code and approved additional package goods licenses on North Noble Street.

### Summary:
The council approved 14 liquor licenses for various restaurants and bars across different districts, including establishments like Neutral Ground, Taco Cinco, Tipsy Scoop, and Chipotle. Three of these licenses required revisions before approval: Zen Thai Cafe, Clarendon Hotel & Spa, and The Green Woodpecker.

The council held a public hearing on the city's property tax increase for the 2025-26 fiscal year. During the hearing, residents raised concerns about whether the tax increase would actually improve fire response times as promised, while others supported the budget's focus on public safety and hiring. The council also adopted several settlement agreements related to claims against the city and approved payment authorizations for vendors and contractors. Public speakers also advocated for installing high-visibility crosswalks to reduce pedestrian fatalities, particularly at the intersection of 46th Avenue and Thomas Road.

### Summary:
The city council approved several infrastructure and service contracts. A $10.5 million contract with RoadSafe Traffic Systems will handle pavement marking work citywide, including installation and maintenance of road markings. The council also approved a $6.4 million marketing contract with Karsh Hagan for Denver International Airport services over three years, with options to extend for two additional years.

The council also approved dedicating several city-owned parcels of land as public rights-of-way in Districts 1 and 7, establishing them officially as streets and public alleys. Additionally, the city accepted a $964,000 grant (split between federal and city funds) with the Regional Transportation District to study transportation improvements along the Welton Street corridor in District 9.

### Summary:
The committee approved a contract increase of $24,000 for a Project Compliance Assistant position, bringing the total contract amount to $72,000 through March 2017.

The committee also moved forward with accepting three grants and one donation for the Recreation and Neighborhoods departments: $5,000 from St. John Providence Health System, $154,000 from Wayne County Parks Millage Fund, $182,000 from another Wayne County Parks Millage Fund allocation, and a $19,250 donation from Quicken Loans (which includes 14 computers and transportation reimbursement funds for community volunteers). ### Summary:
The council approved several contracts and agreements, including deals for investment consulting services for employee retirement plans, grants funding for healthy communities initiatives, updates to city pay and classification rules, an apprenticeship program with Arizona State University and community colleges, and vehicle services for the city. The council also approved a waiver allowing an LKY real estate company to operate a data center with special permit exemptions in District 8, and approved the city's purchase of property at 113 S. 36th Street for Sky Harbor International Airport expansion.

Two items were delayed to the December 3rd meeting: a rezoning request for the Metro North Corporate Park development near Thunderbird Road and Black Canyon Highway, and a waiver request for the Sheely Center to operate a data center. The council also approved a database subscription contract for police license plate readers, but added strict conditions requiring that any data collected be used only for local law enforcement and prohibiting the vendor from selling Phoenix data to third parties or data brokers.

### Summary:
The City Council approved a zoning change for the MTA's 125th Street and Lexington Avenue property in Manhattan. The area will be rezoned to allow for taller, denser commercial development. The council voted 47 in favor of the rezoning application.

The council also considered several measures to support veterans, including a proposed law to provide rental assistance to homeless veterans and another to study a mental health therapy program for veterans. Additionally, the council discussed calling on the state to create property tax breaks for veterans with 100% service-connected disabilities and asking the Housing Authority to give veterans preference in public housing admissions.

### Summary:
The council approved several sign permits at various locations throughout the city, including 3700 S Morgan, 513 S Damen Ave, 2233 W Division St, and two locations on W Diversey Parkway. The council also voted to rezone properties at 2501 N Elston Ave and 2821 N Orchard Ave to allow for different development uses.

The council passed a revised municipal code to strengthen laws against illegal firearm possession and approved a tax incentive deal with 4300 Roosevelt LLC to help fund construction and parking at 4300 W Roosevelt Rd. Additionally, the council designated a Transit Facility Improvement Area for the Red Line Extension Project and made several personnel appointments to the Stockyards Commission.

Mr. Huckins mentioned Ed Zuercher proved he could work with different cities and government entities working at the Maricopa Association of Governments (MAG), especially during negotiations at the Legislature on Prop 479. Mr. Avent recalled one of Ed Zuercher's first jobs after his internship was management assistant in his office where he got to know Ed Zuercher very well. Mr. Avent said Ed Zuercher was smart, a great communicator, cared for people and was fair. Mr. Avent emphasized Ed Zuercher sought ways to say yes when possible, but was also able to say no effectively. Mr. Avent pointed out Ed Zuercher had a proven record as a City Manager and had refined and utilized those skills well. Mr. Avent expressed times were uncertain and the City needed stability at the top of its leadership. Mr. Avent urged Council to approve this item.\par \par Leonard Clark expressed he wanted Ed Zuercher back and remembered he was fair and calm which the City needed.\par \par Chip Mulkins, a board member on the Support Sky Harbor Coalition, spoke in support of this item. He recognized City Manager Jeffrey Barton for his partnership and leadership during his tenure and thanked him for his service. Mr. Mulkins conveyed Ed Zuercher previously guided the City through some challenging and successful times and was confident he would again serve the City with excellence. Mr. Mulkins said he looked forward to working with him and continue to advance their organization's mission.\par \par Maria Tyne, a board member on the Friends of Transit, thanked City Manager Jeffrey Barton for all of his work and years of commitment to the City. She mentioned she worked with Mr. Barton during her time with the City and knew he was an incredible leader. She said she supported Ed Zuercher as she also worked with him as a previous City employee. She stated she saw his commitment towards transit with Transit 2000 and Transportation 2050 as well as his commitment through MAG with Proposition 479. She expressed he would bring that passion back to the City.\par \par Dominic Papa, senior director for the Phoenix Community Alliance, spoke in support to

### Summary:
The Phoenix City Council approved an employment agreement with Ed Zuercher to return as City Manager. The council voted to move forward with the potential agreement after hearing public comment from business and community leaders who praised Zuercher's management experience, communication skills, and proven track record leading the city through previous challenges.

The council also approved a motion to file a petition with the Arizona Court of Appeals seeking special action relief related to airport baggage handling systems operations and maintenance procurement.

### Summary:
The council approved a tax incentive for three properties on South Kedzie Avenue and an intergovernmental agreement with the Regional Transportation Authority to manage curb space and mobility around the CTA's Kimball Street Station in Albany Park, using money from a local tax increment financing fund. They also reappointed Charles K. Newsome to the Hyde Park Commission's Special Service Area No. 61.

The council granted numerous exemptions allowing local businesses to access alleys without physical barriers, including funeral homes, laundromats, event spaces, auto shops, a daycare center, and an animal hospital. They also approved street vacation for Norfolk Southern Railway's 47th Street facility expansion, a permit for Sharks Fish-Chicken, and created an honorary street designation for "Amanda Calo Way."

### Summary:
Seattle's Select Committee on the Comprehensive Plan held a public hearing on April 6, 2026, to discuss proposed changes to the city's zoning and land use rules. The committee reviewed an ordinance that would amend multiple sections of Seattle's zoning code and update the official land use map across numerous city pages and locations.

Residents could provide public comments during two sessions: remote speakers registered between 8:30-10:30 a.m. for the morning session starting at 9:30 a.m., while in-person speakers at City Hall registered between 2:30-6:30 p.m. for the afternoon session at 3:00 p.m. Each speaker was given one minute to comment, though groups of four or more speakers received four minutes total for a group presentation.

### Summary:
The council approved multiple demolition contracts totaling over $400,000, fully funded by the city and state. These include residential demolitions by Farrow Group Inc. ($104,772 and $148,924), emergency demolitions at two specific addresses ($19,695 and $28,800), and hazmat/environmental assessments for commercial demolition projects by PSI and other contractors ($43,300, $29,350, and $35,437).

The council also corrected two contract errors: a WIC and immunization office lease with Samaritan Center was amended to reflect the correct five-year total of $288,361.33 instead of $72,534.23, and a Rocket Fiber utility pole and conduit agreement was clarified to specify it runs for five years from council approval with per-unit pricing. Additionally, the Detroit Health Department received authorization to apply for a $35,000 state grant for health innovation with no local match required.

### Meeting Minutes:
CITY: Denver DATE: 2026-02-25 BODY: Health_and_Safety AGENDA ITEM: Attendance AGENDA ITEM: Briefings AGENDA ITEM: Caring for Denver Foundation Update ACTION: Council Presentation 26-0191 was heard in Committee. NOTES: Lorez Meinhold, Caring for Denver Foundation AGENDA ITEM: Residential Health Task Force Update ACTION: Council Presentation 26-0192 was heard in Committee. NOTES: Danica Lee and Nicol Caldwell, Department of Public Health and Environment AGENDA ITEM: Consent Items AGENDA ITEM: A resolution approving and providing for the execution of a proposed Grant Agreement between the City and County of Denver and the United States of America Department of Health and Human Services concerning the “HIV Emergency Relief Project Grants - Ryan White Part A FY26” program and the funding therefor. Approves a grant with the U.S. Department of Health and Human Services Health Resources and Services Administration for $2,135,483.00 with an end date of 2-28-2027 to provide care, treatment, and supportive services to individuals living with HIV/AIDS in the Denver Transitional Grant Area (TGA) through the 2026 Ryan White Part A Renewal Grant Award (Round 1), citywide (ENVHL-202683364). The last regularly scheduled Council meeting within the 30-day review period is on 3-31-2026. The Committee approved filing this item at its meeting on 2-25-2026. ACTION: Council Resolution 26-0170 was approved by consent. AGENDA ITEM: A resolution approving and providing for the execution of a proposed Grant Agreement between the City and County of Denver and the Caring for Denver Foundation concerning the “Peer Support Supervision and Technical Assistance Hub (The Hub)” program and the funding therefor. Approves a grant agreement with Caring for Denver Foundation for $506,389.00 with an end date of 11-30-2027 to establish a Peer Support Supervision and Technical Assistance Hub that will support activities to boost retention, satisfaction, and service quality among peer support programs operated by the City and County and launch Denver’s first Peer Support Registered Apprenticeship Program expanding the workforce pipeline; citywide (ENVHL-202683169). The last regularly scheduled Council meeting within the 30-day review period is on 3-31-2026. The Committee approved filing this item at its meeting on 2-25-2026. ACTION: Council Resolution 26-0171 was approved by consent.

### Summary:
The Health and Safety Committee approved two grant agreements. The city received $2.1 million from the federal Department of Health and Human Services to provide HIV/AIDS care, treatment, and support services to Denver residents through early 2027. Additionally, the city approved a $506,000 grant with the Caring for Denver Foundation to create a peer support hub that will improve job retention and satisfaction among peer support workers, while also launching Denver's first peer support apprenticeship program to train new workers.

Both resolutions were approved and will move forward to the full city council for a final vote by March 31, 2026. These funds will support health services and workforce development across Denver.

She continued staff recommends approval of Item 82 per the September 17, 2025 memo from the Planning and Development Director and adopt the related ordinance.\par \par Councilwoman Hodge Washington said she wanted to hear the applicant first as some concerns were addressed.\par \par NOTE: Mayor Gallego returned to the voting body and resumed as Chair.\par \par Brian Greathouse, representing Taylor Morrison, recalled Council's request two weeks ago and held two meetings with neighbors so they could obtain information about this case. He emphasized 11 people attended the first meeting and 7 people attended the second meeting, noting there were 6 people in attendance who had not seen their presentation. He stated during those two meetings people raised concerns and he tried to find solutions as well as communicated with the District 8 Office. He said his client accepts the stipulations in the revised memo, specifically the following:\par \pard{\pntext\f2\'B7\tab}{\*\pn\pnlvlblt\pnf2\pnindent0{\pntxtb\'B7}}\fi-360\li360 Reduction in units to 175;\par {\pntext\f2\'B7\tab}Additional landscape buffers and hedge around the perimeter to address privacy concerns;\par {\pntext\f2\'B7\tab}Reduce the perimeter building heights to single-story to eliminate second-story windows;\par {\pntext\f2\'B7\tab}Plant a minimum of one tree in every backyard around the perimeter of the site; and\par {\pntext\f2\'B7\tab}Eliminate site access on Baseline Road to right-in and right-out to address traffic concerns.\par \pard\par Mr. Greathouse requested Council approve the items, noting he had a presentation available that was shown at the last meeting.\par \par Councilwoman Hodge Washington thanked him for his testimony and requested the appellant provide comments.\par \par Brittany Conklin stated this matter was continued two weeks ago with direction the community meet with the applicant's representatives to discuss this project, address concerns and come to a potential compromise. She conveyed the applicant held two virtual meetings, but not all questions asked by community members were answered. She listed the following community feedback after the two meetings:\par \pard{\pntext\f2\'B7\tab}{\*\pn\pnlvlblt\pnf2\pnindent0{\pntxtb\'B7}}\fi-360\li360 Applicant advised the community's request for R-2 zoning was not financially beneficial for them;\par {\pntext\f2\'B7\tab}Commercial center was not approached for a secondary point of egress;\par {\pntext\f2\'B7\tab}Map of the area does not take into consideration traffic from other approved housing;\par {\pntext\f2\'B7\tab}Daily traffic was higher than presented, noting the Traffic Count Database System reported there were 49,898 vehicles in 2024;\par {\pntext\f2\'B7\tab}Online and door-to-door outreach continued by the applicant; and\par {\pntext\f2\'B7\tab}Additional 30+ letters were signed in opposition.\par \pard\par Ms. Conklin remarked traffic and safety remain the key issue as Baseline Road was a major hazard with growth compounding it. She pointed out the applicant was unable to address traffic concerns and showed a rollover accident at the intersection where this proposal would be located. She mentioned there were other community concerns, such as blackouts which was a safety issue during the summer. She claimed an anonymous home inspector informed her that many new homebuilders, including the applicant, do not construct to Arizona minimum standards. She said that was important due to one point of egress which would become a safety hazard in an emergency. She emphasized there was strong opposition to this project, noting the Village Planning Committee recommended less density. He recalled that opposition asked for 150 units with the halfway point between

### Summary:
The council voted to approve two development items for a residential project. Item 81 passed 7-4, and Item 82 was denied as originally filed but approved 6-5 with modifications. The developer, Taylor Morrison, agreed to reduce the project from potentially 219 units down to 175 units, limit building heights to single-story on the perimeter to eliminate second-story windows, add landscape buffers and trees for privacy, and restrict traffic access on Baseline Road to right-in and right-out turns only.

Community opposition remains focused on traffic and safety concerns on Baseline Road, which residents say is already a hazard. The appellant argued the applicant failed to fully address community questions, presented lower traffic counts than actual data shows, and rejected requests for lower density zoning (R-2) and an additional exit point into a nearby shopping center. Residents also raised concerns about power outages, construction quality standards, and emergency evacuation with only one access road. The community asked council to deny the rezoning if the developer wouldn't meet their additional demands.

### Summary:
The council approved three rezoning ordinances for District 6 and District 7, allowing land use changes at specific locations including the northeast corner of 17th Street and Northern Avenue. Two other rezoning requests were postponed to November 5, 2025. Council members praised community involvement in the South Central area development, particularly around the new Ed Pastor Center and upcoming light rail line, with emphasis on supporting small local businesses.

The council also voted to approve or adopt a large batch of items (items 24-114) related to liquor licenses, permits, and other municipal matters, with several items excluded or postponed. One councilwoman was absent, and one attended virtually.

### Summary:
The city council approved multiple zoning changes across various neighborhoods. These changes allow property owners at 15 different locations throughout the city to modify how their land can be used — potentially enabling new types of development or businesses in those areas. Locations affected include sites on Division Street, Elm Street, Halsted Street, Damen Avenue, Elston Avenue, and several others across different parts of the city.

The council also approved signage permits for a property at 3700 S Morgan Street on both the north and south sides of the building. All of these items passed after Alderman Tunney made the motions, with Alderman Cappleman attempting unsuccessfully to reconsider some of the votes.
