### Summary:
The Phoenix City Council approved several board and commission appointments, including Robbin Coulon to the Civil Service Board, Mark Moeremans and Tina Marie Tentori to the Industrial Development Authority, Kate Bauer to the Paradise Valley Village Planning Committee, Leon Thomas to the Parks and Recreation Board, and David Matthews as Chair of the Deferred Compensation Board.

The council also approved liquor licenses for ten new establishments across various districts, including breweries, sports bars, and restaurants like Huss Brewpub, El Corral Sports Bar, and Tipsy Scoop. Additionally, the council approved four special event liquor licenses for organizations including the Hawker Foundation, Alwun House Foundation, Asian Chamber of Commerce, and Phoenix Law Enforcement Association Charities.

### Summary:
The City Council voted on four tax-related laws on December 18, 2025. They approved a land bank creation bill, a law allowing the city to sell tax liens, a requirement for notice to condo boards when tax liens are sold, and a law about reporting unresolved tax liens. Three of the four bills passed with strong majorities (41-47 votes in favor), while one passed by consent. Seven council members voted against the first two bills, but nearly unanimous support emerged for the tax lien reporting measure.

These laws affect property owners and buildings with unpaid taxes. The land bank and tax lien sales give the city new tools to manage properties with tax debt, while the notice requirement ensures condo boards are informed when their buildings' tax liens are sold. The reporting law creates oversight of unresolved tax cases.

### Summary:
The Board of Supervisors approved several items on October 25, 2022. They proclaimed October 23-29 as Asexual Awareness Week, appointed Uzuri Pease-Greene to the Eastern Neighborhoods Citizens Advisory Committee, and declared October 27 as SF Spikes Day to recognize the soccer organization's work with the LGBTQ+ community. They also postponed a resolution about pushing the state legislature for stricter penalties on illegal street racing and stunt driving.

Public speakers raised concerns about transphobic comments from elected officials, traffic safety issues, co-op housing and support services, and access to public comment at board meetings. The board also approved allowing teleconferenced meetings during emergencies and authorized a settlement payment of $203,885 related to a lawsuit against the federal Department of Health and Human Services.

### Summary:
Council approved funding increases for two emergency services projects. They expanded a contract with Mission Critical Partners by $378,907 to continue managing Denver's 911 dispatch and records systems through the end of 2029, and approved a $24.3 million, 13-year contract with Motorola Solutions to provide computer-aided dispatch software for emergency responders citywide.

### Summary:
The council approved two contracts to help homeless people with intellectual and developmental disabilities get housing. Jewish Family Service of Colorado will receive $3.42 million through April 2029 to provide rapid rehousing services for at least 55 households. Rocky Mountain Human Services will get $2.58 million through the same date to provide stability navigation services—helping coordinate support—for at least 300 people and families experiencing homelessness or housing insecurity.

### Meeting Minutes:
\viewkind4\uc1\pard\cf1\fs28 City Manager Jeffrey Barton acknowledged the Phoenix City Council\rquote s leadership and vision in navigating the Fiscal Year (FY) 2025-26 budget amid a challenging economic climate marked by funding cuts and reduced consumer spending. He recognized their commitment to preserving essential services without reducing those relied upon by residents.\par \par Budget and Research Director Amber Williamson reported that the FY26 budget was approximately $6.9 billion, comprised of three main funding sources including: the General Fund which represents 32 percent and supports core services such as public safety; Special Revenue Funds which represents 33 percent and supports voter-approved initiatives such as the Parks and Preserve Initiative and Transportation 2050; and Enterprise Funds, which represents 35 percent and includes departments that generate their own revenue.\par \par Ms. Williamson noted that the projected FY2026 General Fund budget was approximately $2.2 billion. Roughly 62 percent of expenditures are allocated to public, 11 percent for community enrichment,11 percent for general government, 7 percent allocated to criminal justice, community development, environmental services, and transportation, and 9 percent for contingency and other needs. Revenues are primarily derived from local sales and excise taxes and represent 43 percent of incoming revenue, 35 percent are state-shared revenues, 12 percent is primary property tax, and 10 percent of revenue came from user fees or other sources.\par \par Ms. Williamson reviewed the FY2026 General Fund status, noting an anticipated $39 million deficit. She attributed the shortfall to state actions that reduced revenue and discussed the impact of eliminating the residential rental tax and the reduction of the individual income tax rate to 2.5 percent, along with rising operating costs.\par \par Ms. Williamson outlined some key budget considerations including: enhancing fire emergency services to reduce response times; addressing homelessness to support unsheltered individuals and mitigate neighborhood impacts due to the expiration of American Rescue Plan Act funds; operating costs associated with the 2023 General Obligation (GO) Bond Program and planning for future GO Bond capacity; slowing General Fund revenues and lower projections; potential legislative actions that could further restrict local control, reduce revenues, and increase expenditures. Ms. Williamson emphasized that pending legislation, if enacted, could have an annual impact exceeding $50 million.\par \par Ms. Williamson outlined strategies to balance the General Fund budget. She noted this included the approval of a Transaction Privilege Tax (TPT) increase from 2.3 percent to 2.8 percent, effective July 1, 2025. Ms. Williamson said this measure was approved on March 18, 2025, and offsets revenue losses from state actions and safeguards existing programs and services. She added that the approved excise tax bond sale would provide funding for public safety capital costs, reducing the cash burden on the General Fund. Of the revenue generated, $92 million from the TPT increase will be reserved to help balance the FY 2026 budget. In addition, City departments were directed to reprioritize existing General Fund budgets, yielding $24 million in savings without impacting services. These reductions came from vacant positions, contractual commodities, and miscellaneous expenditures.\par \par Ms. Williamson stated that the proposed FY 2026 budget includes several supplementals. This includes $34 million to reallocate 32 sworn SAFER positions into the General Fund, funding for 24 sworn fire positions to support the opening of GO Bond Fire Station 15, and continued funding for the Office of Homeless Solutions. She further noted that an additional ongoing $25 million was proposed for the Fire Department which would fund 134 sworn and 19 civilian positions, aimed at reducing emergency response times. As a result of this investment, the sworn hiring target would increase from 1,865 to 2,023.\par \par Ms. Williamson explained that if the in the strategies mentioned were implemented, the General Fund Status would result in a $17 million surplus. Ms. Williamson stated the City Manager was recommending the surplus be set aside to address significant uncertainty such as tariffs, trade, taxes, legislative action, federal funding cuts, inflation, etc. \par \par Additionally, Ms. Williamson noted the budget includes the conversion of 38.6 full-time critical positions which would not require additional funds and would use existing department resources. It would also include $2 million in ARPA interest to the Housing Trust Fund to address efforts for affordable funding. Additionally, Ms. Williamson said that a $5.6 million Non-General Fund supplemental would be added to reopen the Cave Creek Water Reclamation Plant to expand capacity for wastewater treatment and water purification, adding 28 full-time positions and 20 vehicles. \par \par Ms. Williamson stated the Budget and Research Department hosted 12 community budget hearings and received comments via phone and email. Together, 440 comments from 402 residents showed support for the proposed budget. Ms. Williamson said the next steps of the budget process include the City Council budget decision on May 21, followed by the legal budget adoption on June 4, legal budget adoption on June 18,

### Summary:
Phoenix is facing a $39 million budget shortfall due to state funding cuts and rising costs. To balance the budget, the city approved a sales tax increase from 2.3% to 2.8% (effective July 1, 2025), which will bring in $92 million. The city also found $24 million in savings by cutting vacant positions and other expenses without reducing services. If these strategies work as planned, the city projects a $17 million surplus, which the manager wants to hold in reserve for unexpected costs.

The budget includes major investments in fire services: $25 million to hire 158 new firefighters and civilian positions to reduce emergency response times. The city is also adding $34 million to reallocate fire positions, funding 24 new firefighters for a new fire station, and continuing homeless services. Additionally, $2 million will go to affordable housing, and $5.6 million will reopen the Cave Creek Water Reclamation Plant. The City Council will vote on the budget May 21 with final adoption in June.

CITY: Denver DATE: 2026-03-17 BODY: Governance_and_Intergovernmental_Relations AGENDA ITEM: Attendance AGENDA ITEM: Action Items AGENDA ITEM: A bill for an ordinance amending Chapter 13, article II of the DRMC to require that contracts, grants, and intergovernmental agreements be filed at the same time as the bill or resolution approving the agreement is filed. To sign up to speak visit www.denvergov.org/councilpublicinput. a) Briefing b) Public comment c) Discussion/Action AGENDA ITEM: A resolution amending City Council's Rules of Procedure. ACTION: A motion offered by Vice Chair Kashmann, duly seconded by Council member Gonzales-Gutierrez, that Council Bill 26-0327 and Council Resolution 26-0329 be approved for filing in a block, carried by the following vote: AGENDA ITEM: Briefings AGENDA ITEM: State Legislative Update ACTION: Council Presentation 26-0330 was heard in Committee. NOTES: Adam Paul, Mayor's Office Heather Retzko, Katie Hancock, Tanya Kelly-Bowry, and Ximena Luna, Policy Matters Maria Garcia Berry and Jamie Lewis, RTD Accountability Task Force Council members Sawyer and Gonzales-Gutierrez

### Summary:
The city council committee approved two procedural changes to improve government transparency and accountability. First, they required that contracts, grants, and intergovernmental agreements must be filed at the same time as the bills or resolutions approving them—keeping the paperwork together. Second, they amended council rules to require draft agreements be shared with council members before those agreements are discussed in committee meetings. Both items will move forward for a full council hearing with 15 minutes of public comment available.

The committee also received a briefing on state legislative updates and heard a presentation on RTD (transit authority) accountability from a task force.

### Summary:
The council unanimously approved proclamations recognizing May 2025 as ALS Association Month and June 19, 2025 as Juneteenth. During the Juneteenth proclamation presentation, organizer Tasha White raised concerns that the city recently stopped covering staffing costs for event organizers and asked the council to formally recognize Juneteenth as a city-assisted civic event with the same support given to other longstanding community celebrations.

The council voted 4-2 to extend the public comment period beyond the standard 30 minutes to allow 19 residents to speak for three minutes each. Several speakers, including representatives from the Juneteenth EbonyFest and Highland Festival, asked the city for financial support and resources to help their community events continue and grow.

### Summary:
The Seattle Select Committee on the Library Levy discussed a proposal to ask voters to approve a property tax increase for the city's library system. The tax would last up to seven years and fund expanded library hours, book and technology collections, programming, maintenance, and a seismic retrofit of one library building. The committee held a briefing and discussion on the measure, which is scheduled to go before voters on August 4, 2026.

No final vote or decision was recorded in these meeting notes. The committee recessed briefly during the roughly 90-minute meeting but did not document any amendments or actions taken on the library levy proposal.

### Summary:
The committee approved changes to how criminal history is considered when issuing business licenses in Chicago. They also approved allowing additional liquor licenses for package goods stores on portions of N Noble Street, W 63rd Street, S Homan Avenue, and N Lincoln Avenue.

The committee blocked new amusement venue licenses in certain areas and designated the 23rd Precinct of the 13th Ward as a restricted residential zone, which will prevent additional shared housing units and vacation rentals from being added there.

### Summary:
The city presented accomplishments from its digital accessibility and inclusion initiatives over the past 14 months. Key projects include integrating AI-powered translation tools in libraries (supporting 220+ languages), installing six accessible digital kiosks downtown, advancing Phoenix Sky Harbor's accessibility features, and launching "Accessible Phoenix," a centralized resource hub for city services. The city also held events like the Innovate Phoenix Challenge (drawing 150 participants focused on accessibility in public spaces) and a Venture Café hackathon, with plans for another challenge in January 2026.

Beyond digital efforts, the city is expanding physical accessibility across parks with barrier-free designs, accessible playgrounds, and wheelchair-friendly trails. Public transit improvements include enhanced accessibility at the 50 Street Light Rail Station and specialized services like Dial-A-Ride. The city is also working toward Certified Autism Center status, with 2,200 employees trained so far, and a career program that has resulted in 477 job offers. A Disability Awareness and Recognition Event is scheduled for October 23, 2025, to honor community members and organizations supporting inclusion.

### Summary:
A new apartment development on Peoria Avenue faced traffic concerns from the nearby Tapatio Cove HOA. The HOA wanted to limit traffic through their community by restricting westbound entry onto Peoria Avenue and eastbound entry from Peoria into the complex. The developer agreed to install a traffic control device at the main entry on Peoria Avenue to direct outgoing traffic toward Cave Creek Road, though questions remained about controlling incoming traffic from the west.

During citizen comments, a longtime Piestewa Peak resident asked the city to halt construction on a park improvement project due to lack of public notice and concerns about a 140-foot bridge and other features affecting the mountain preserve's natural appearance. A resident near Central Arizona Shelter Services also complained about trash pickup happening at 3 a.m. causing noise disturbances, and requested help fully closing alleys in her neighborhood.

### Summary:
The council voted to approve four zoning changes across Denver neighborhoods. One property in Northeast Park Hill at 3601 North Monaco Street will be rezoned to allow different development. A Cherry Creek property at 2625 East 3rd Avenue was rezoned to a commercial neighborhood classification. Multiple properties across Bear Valley, College View, Harvey Park, and Marston in the Far Southwest were rezoned to allow mixed-use development. A property at 4211 North Hooker Street in Berkeley was also rezoned for mixed-use purposes.

The council announced three upcoming public hearings scheduled for May 4, 2026, on additional zoning changes for properties on North Knox Court in Villa Park and North Wabash Street in East Colfax, as well as amendments to the city's site development plan approval process. Residents have until April 27 to file any formal objections to these proposals. The council also approved a technical change requiring that contracts and agreements be filed at the same time as the bills approving them.

### Summary:
The council approved several service contracts for the city, including engineering services for a transportation study in districts 3, 4, and 6; architectural services through 2028; and equipment purchases. They also passed a motion to adopt a middle housing ordinance citywide that allows more diverse housing types like duplexes and townhomes. The council will partner with Arizona State University to study how to expand middle housing in more neighborhoods, assess impacts on infrastructure, and develop strategies to keep these units affordable, with results due in one year.

A separate rezoning request for property at 99th Avenue and Thomas Road was postponed to December 3rd. Multiple residents spoke against the middle housing ordinance during public comment, though the council approved it anyway.

### Summary:
The Phoenix City Council approved 13 liquor license applications on February 4, 2026, including licenses for restaurants, bars, and meat markets across various districts, as well as special event licenses for organizations like Rosie's House: A Music Academy for Children and local churches.

The council also adopted several ordinances affecting city operations citywide, including changes to employee pay classifications, amendments to family leave administration services, and approval for the city to accept easements for drainage, water, sewer, and utility purposes in several districts. A zoning request for property at the southwest corner of 35th Avenue and Broadway Road was postponed until February 18.

### Summary:
The council proposed a new rule requiring apartment buildings with elevators to keep at least one elevator working for residents to use. The proposal is under review by the Land Use and Transportation Committee.

The city also approved accepting multiple state grants totaling nearly $4.6 million to fund law enforcement and victim services programs through 2026, including wage theft enforcement, elder abuse support, victim assistance, and outreach to underserved communities. These grants were sent to the Budget and Finance Committee for final approval.

### Summary:
The City Council approved a zoning change in Queens that allows a commercial overlay district at 18-15 Francis Lewis Boulevard, changing the area from residential zoning to mixed-use zoning. The vote was 47 in favor and 1 against. The council also filed a sidewalk café application for a restaurant at 37 Canal Street in Manhattan after the applicant withdrew their request.

The council passed a new law restricting where mobile syringe service programs can distribute needles and syringes, with 44 voting in favor, 3 opposed, and 1 abstaining. This law limits these harm-reduction services from operating in certain locations across the city.

(Reference as contained in Exhibit Book 2 to Minute Book 114 at Page 38) J.2: Public Hearing – Unified Development Ordinance Amendment (File #202400414) Subject hearing involves a request to amend Section 7.1-1 Table of Uses of the Unified Development Ordinance to allow for the use of a Dwelling, Single-family Attached by right in the urban core zoning districts. Presenter: Ms. Maddy Gates, Senior Planner Mayor Franks opened the Public Hearing and recognized Ms. Maddy Gates, Senior Planner. Ms. Gates presented information on the proposed Unified Development Ordinance Amendment to allow for the use of a dwelling, single-family attached (townhomes) by right in the Urban Core Zoning Districts. Ms. Gates explained that the City is experiencing an increase in infill requests for smaller townhome projects in the Urban Core Zoning Districts. She said this proposed text amendment would allow for -56- Page 57 Page 57 REGULAR CITY COUNCIL MEETING – SEPTEMBER 17, 2024 townhomes by-right in the Urban Industrial (I-U), Urban Mixed-Use (UMU), and Central Business District (CBD) and would result in a high-quality product that blends convenience and community in urban Gastonia. Ms. Gates explained the text amendment would promote walkability and density in proximity to downtown, civic amenities, parks and recreational facilities, and social gathering spaces. Ms. Gates said staff and Planning Commission recommend approval of the proposed amendment. Ms. Gates presented a map of the Urban Gastonia Zoning Districts, a copy of which is included in the exhibits. Ms. Gates responded to questions. There was no one signed up to speak during the Public Hearing. Councilwoman Barber made a motion to close the Public Hearing and approve the proposed text amendment to the Unified Development Ordinance as presented. This Ordinance is consistent with the Gastonia 2025 Comprehensive Plan and any applicable duly adopted small area plans and is reasonable and in the public’s interest. Councilman Odom seconded the motion. The proposed amendment was approved by a vote of 5–1 with Councilwoman Stepp dissenting. (Reference as contained in Exhibit Book 2 to Minute Book 114 at Page 39) J.3: Consideration of a Resolution Approving a Change in the Holiday Schedule for the City of Gastonia Presenter: Mr. Michael C. Peoples, City Manager City Manager Michael Peoples presented information on the proposed change in the holiday schedule for the City of Gastonia. He explained that if the City adopts the same schedule as the State of North Carolina beginning with the upcoming 2025 calendar year, then Friday, December 26, 2025, would be recognized as a third day at Christmas because of Christmas Day falling on Thursday that year. Mr. Peoples said the State schedule recognizes a floating holiday which currently exists on the holiday schedule for the City of Gastonia. Mr. Peoples said if the City adopts the same schedule as the State, there would be 12 recognized holidays and one floating holiday. Discussion ensued. Councilman Koutsoupias made a motion to approve the proposed change in the holiday schedule for the City of Gastonia to adopt the same schedule as that of the State of North Carolina. Councilwoman Stepp seconded the motion. The motion passed with a vote of 5-1 with Councilwoman Barber dissenting. ### Summary:
The city council approved changes to zoning rules that will allow townhomes to be built by right in three downtown areas: Urban Industrial, Urban Mixed-Use, and Central Business District zones. This move aims to increase housing options and walkability in Gastonia's urban core by making it easier for developers to build smaller townhome projects near downtown, parks, and community spaces. The vote passed 5-1, with only Councilwoman Stepp dissenting.

The council also voted to align the city's holiday schedule with North Carolina's state schedule starting in 2025. This means city employees will observe 12 regular holidays plus one floating holiday each year. The change passed 5-1, with Councilwoman Barber as the sole dissenting vote.

### Summary:
The council held public hearings on two commercial rehabilitation projects. The first involves establishing a Commercial Rehabilitation District for Coe Van Dyke, LLC in an area near Van Dyke Street between Agnes and Coe Streets. The Planning Department found the request meets state requirements and aligns with the city's development goals. The second hearing covered a tax exemption request from VG Statler City, LLC for a commercial rehabilitation project in the Park Avenue area downtown.

The council also received a presentation from the Downtown Detroit Partnership about the Downtown Business Improvement Zone. Additional agenda items included City Planning Commission, Historic Designation Advisory Board, and Housing and Revitalization Department matters, though details on those items were not provided in this text.

### Summary:
The council approved a rezoning at 21st Place and Eugie Terrace (District 3) to allow a 24-unit apartment building with four stories and ground-level parking. A neighbor who has lived nearby for 42 years opposed the project over concerns about building height, privacy from balconies, and parking overflow onto streets. The council voted to approve it, noting the developer could build similarly tall structures under current zoning, and citing Phoenix's need for more housing.

The council also held a public hearing on a rezoning at 17th Street and Griswold Road (District 6) for low-scale office use. The applicant proposed a 15-foot building designed as a transition between commercial and residential areas, and made changes to address neighborhood concerns. The council voted to approve this rezoning as well.

She mentioned she was a City employee during the recession and her department decreased from over 500 staff to 280 people, but no one lost their job because City employees made sacrifices to support each other. She pointed out Ed Zuercher was part of that leadership and led the charge in trying to reinstate employee benefits which showed he cared about staff. She stated Ed Zuercher provided stability, noting the City already had great leadership in the management team but he was the right person at this time.\par \par NOTE: Councilwoman Guardado returned to the voting body and Councilman Robinson temporarily left the voting body during Councilwoman Stark's comments.\par \par Councilman Waring explained his involvement in this process was a councilperson called him about Ed Zuercher's interest in coming back, so he called Mr. Zuercher and confirmed that was correct. Councilman Waring recalled when the current City Manager was hired in 2021 he had one lengthy conversation about it with Councilwoman Pastor, so this was a similar situation. He emphasized an individual needed to have a special skill set to be a City Manager, noting it was just as hard to find someone with the right skills to run the Phoenix airport or oversee the Police or Fire departments.\par \par Councilman Waring remarked he worked with Ed Zuercher when he was the City Manager for eight years and was confident he could do the job. Councilman Waring said he understood there was staff who could do the job, but some leadership staff were leaving the City including Community and Economic Development Director Chris Mackay who brought in many projects that benefited Phoenicians. He expressed this was the right hire at this time which he also told The Arizona Republic as well as laid out why this decision made sense. He stated Ed Zuercher was responsive to him and would explain when his answer was no, noting some of the earlier questions related to actions that could be illegal and appreciated he was non-committal in his answers as it would be inappropriate. Councilman Waring conveyed the City could be facing some difficult times, but Ed Zuercher was with the City during the recession and guided Phoenix through it.\par \par NOTE: Councilman Robinson returned to the voting body during Councilman Waring's comments.\par \par Councilwoman Hodge Washington remarked she did not have an opportunity to work with Ed Zuercher in the past, noting her concerns related to the process so she wanted to confirm some things with him if this was the format for their decision. She asked what he would do different this time as the Council composition was dissimilar and the City did not have the same needs.\par \par Mr. Zuercher replied what he did would be determined by the circumstances, but he was committed to being flexible and work from the present with current options. He stated the bigger picture was the key to any success was investing in City employees. He stressed a lot of decisions were made by a City Manager and there were some people who did not care for those decisions. He recalled when he was City Manager he negotiated contracts that took pay and benefits away from employees, but he also negotiated the return of pay and benefits with additional pay some time later. He remarked those decisions were made to preserve services and employment which was a high priority for Council. He expressed he had a deeper appreciation of how critical employees were to the City's success and would invest in them.\par \par Councilwoman Hodge Washington stated if he were rehired he would be entering an environment with low employee morale due to this process. She questioned how he would strengthen employee relationships.\par \par Mr. Zuercher recalled when he started as the City Manager in 2013 about 40-50 percent of the department head and Manager's Office positions were either acting, interim or vacant. ### Summary:
City council members discussed and advocated for rehiring Ed Zuercher as City Manager. Multiple council members praised Zuercher's past performance, noting his experience navigating the recession, his knowledge of the city's financial and organizational systems, and his commitment to city employees. They emphasized that losing experienced leaders like the current manager and other key staff meant the city needed someone who could step in quickly without interruption to city operations.

Councilwoman Hodge Washington raised concerns about the hiring process and asked Zuercher how he would approach the job differently given the current council composition and different city needs. Zuercher responded that he would adapt to current circumstances and prioritize investing in city employees to improve morale. He referenced his past experience filling vacant management positions and negotiating employee contracts during the recession to preserve jobs and services.

### Summary:
Council approved a $2.58 million contract with Rocky Mountain Human Services to help people with intellectual and developmental disabilities who are homeless or facing housing insecurity. The program will serve at least 300 people citywide through 2029.

Council also approved spending increases for city operations: $3 million more for portable toilet rental services (bringing that contract to $10 million total through 2028), and up to $15.1 million to renew the city's property and liability insurance policies starting May 1. Additionally, the Mayor made several appointments to city boards, including members to the Crime Prevention and Control Commission and Denver Health and Hospital Authority Board.

### Summary:
The Phoenix City Council approved several contracts and projects affecting residents citywide. These included a cooperative contract for mobility and vehicle conversion services, a road surface sealing contract, a vehicle battery purchase for city operations, and authorization to amend a study agreement for the Mayo Flyover project in District 2. The council also approved a property matter for Sky Harbor International Airport and changed a street name from 40th Street to Crown Canyon Court in District 6.

The council voted to approve most items on the agenda, though some liquor license and other items were excluded from the vote or postponed to a future December 3rd meeting. Residents can watch council meetings online through the city website or Phoenix Channel 11, call in to listen, or attend in person at City Hall, with Spanish interpretation available for all meetings.

### Summary:
The city council approved several contracts and agreements affecting Denver residents. A $150 million contract expansion with United HealthCare will continue providing health insurance to city employees through the end of 2027 with updated coverage for 2026. The council also approved $9.75 million in federal and local funding for the Alameda Avenue Underpass improvement project (between Kalamath and Cherokee streets) in Council District 7, focusing on design and environmental work for eventual reconstruction. Additionally, the city signed a $6.5 million contract with Cutler Repaving to resurface 95 lane miles of streets citywide using recycling treatment.

In other approvals, Dream Center Denver will operate programming at the former Globeville Recreation Center for three years under a $90,000 agreement, and the city secured $2 million in state grant funding for the My Outdoor Colorado program through 2030 serving Council Districts 3 and 9. The council also moved forward on rezoning three properties in the Globeville area and one property in Five Points; public hearings on these zoning changes are scheduled for May 18, 2026.

### Summary:
The council discussed a liquor license application for Ash Lounge and decided to deny it. Key issues included: the business has been operating without a valid use permit since 2016 (when the city incorrectly determined the previous use was abandoned), it violates state spacing requirements by being near a daycare and residential area, and a recent serious incident where a performer was kidnapped and assaulted by someone who held them for three to four days. The business also has a history of problems including drug use, prostitution, and fighting.

Councilwoman Pastor moved to deny the license, citing the invalid permit, proximity to a childcare facility, the violent incident, and ongoing safety concerns in the neighborhood. Councilwoman Stark seconded the motion. The hired attorneys for the business disputed some claims and are working to appeal the 2016 permit decision, but the council voted to reject the liquor license application.

### Summary:
Denver City Council approved several contracts and agreements affecting airport operations, affordable housing, and food services. The airport will pay $6.4 million to Karsh Hagan for marketing and communications services over three years, and $6.8 million to HDR Engineering for design services on a maintenance facility expansion. The city is also investing $7.5 million in a loan to Archway Investment Corporation to build 150 affordable rental units on West 10th Avenue.

The council also increased funding for meal services at Denver's micro-communities, adding $851,000 to The Salvation Army's contract and $950,000 to Work Options for Women's contract, both extending through the end of 2026. Additionally, the council dedicated two city-owned parcels as public streets: North Jason Street near West 37th Avenue and a public alley bounded by South Washington, East Cedar, South Clarkson, and East Bayaud avenues.

### Summary:
The Finance Committee approved multiple development projects using Tax Increment Financing (TIF) funds, which redirect property tax revenue to specific areas. Major projects approved include: a new park with fieldhouse at 4800 S Western Ave, a grocery store in the historic Gerber Building on North Broadway, construction at Roosevelt Road, improvements for the University of Illinois Discovery Partners Institute, a new Near South High School at 2450 S State St, and extensions to the Red Line transit project. The committee also approved fee waivers for the Chicago Board of Education's building projects.

The committee approved several other measures including updates to existing TIF projects in the Kinzie Industrial and Pulaski Corridor areas, designated bank depositories for the city and school board for 2023, and settled three lawsuits against the city and police officers. They also approved various small claims payments and permitted the American Civil Liberties Union to conduct a fundraising tag day.

### Summary:
The Board of Supervisors approved several spending and housing initiatives. The city will lease a property at 2177 Jerrold Avenue for $2.5 million annually to operate a temporary shelter, with the city contributing up to $5.9 million in upfront renovation costs over a 15-year agreement. Additionally, the city approved two loans totaling $39 million to finance affordable housing projects: $24 million for a senior housing building at Great Highway and $15 million for an affordable apartment building at 650 Divisadero Street.

The board also authorized the Department of Juvenile Probation to accept a $360,570 state grant for foster care and family services, and adopted a two-year fixed budget cycle for the Airport, Port, and Public Utilities Commission for 2024-2026. The city is also applying for state grants totaling over $2.3 million for transitional housing and housing navigation programs.

### Summary:
The council discussed Valley Metro bus service hours. Ms. Carver clarified that while the official last bus time is 11 p.m., buses actually continue running their final routes until completion, which can extend well past midnight—some routes run as late as 1 a.m. Buses continue to pick up and drop off passengers normally during these late routes. The city is working with Valley Metro on a comprehensive operational analysis that will take 18 months to two years to complete, reviewing ridership trends and identifying improvements across all routes.

A resident and attorney, Dianne Post, spoke during public comment about concerns with a free speech zone draft policy, saying it contains unconstitutional language. She requested the city create a small work group with civil rights attorneys to rewrite the policy properly and avoid future lawsuits. The mayor acknowledged the concern and said a follow-up would be provided, though the council noted this issue would need to be formally scheduled on a future agenda for proper discussion.

### Summary:
The City Council introduced several proposals aimed at improving transparency and services across city agencies. One proposal would create an online tool letting parents compare school funding and spending across different schools. Another would require the Department of Education to report on dress codes in city schools. A third would establish an office dedicated to Puerto Rico-New York City affairs, and another would require housing developers who receive city funding to cover repair costs for 10 years after construction.

Additional proposals focused on public safety and social services. One would improve health screenings for people arrested at central booking. Another would require the police department to publish crime statistics for public housing developments on its website. A final proposal would stop the Department of Homeless Services from requiring children to be present when families apply for shelter at intake centers.

### Summary:
The council approved 19 zoning reclassifications across Chicago neighborhoods, including areas on the North Side (Addison, Cornelia, Cortez streets), South Side (Michigan Ave, Drexel Ave, Greenwood Ave, Wentworth Ave), and West Side (Fairfield Ave, Marshall Blvd, Kilpatrick Ave). Each zoning change was passed on motions by Alderman Tunney, with unsuccessful attempts to reconsider the votes by Alderman Cappleman.

The zoning changes allow different uses of land at these 19 locations, such as potentially allowing new types of development or buildings. Residents in these neighborhoods should check with the city if they own property nearby, as zoning changes can affect what can be built or how land is used in their area.

### Summary:
The city council unanimously approved committee assignments for 2023-2024. The city attorney reported that NC Gas House Gang, LLC filed for bankruptcy in Maryland, which affects the ballpark. A preliminary injunction hearing scheduled for the next day was postponed, and the city is working with outside counsel to secure the ballpark while continuing discussions with the Atlantic League about the 2024 baseball season.

The city manager reported ongoing projects including a roof replacement at the Garland Center and new equipment for the Fire Department, including a hazmat truck and ladder truck. The Municipal Citizens' Academy will begin in January 2024 with 19 applicants. Council members also announced the grand opening of Gastonian Hall, a new event and entertainment center on Main Street, scheduled for Thursday, December 21, from 5 p.m. to 10 p.m., and highlighted recent community events including the Christmas parade and airport fuel station ribbon-cutting.

### Summary:
The city is receiving two grants for public safety: $35,000 from a Health Innovation Grant with no matching funds required, and an additional $10,314 for the auto theft prevention program, bringing that total budget to $86,444 to fund innovative programs addressing car theft and fraud.

The council is deferring demolition orders on eight properties around the city (on Montrose, Lumpkin, Coram, Harrison, Hazelwood, McClellan, and two others) after inspections found the buildings are secured and structurally sound enough to repair. Each property gets a six-month reprieve from demolition. The council also asked the Law Department to review whether the city can revoke business licenses and land use permits when repeated illegal activity occurs at a location.

### Summary:
The City Council introduced four bills focused on improving internet access in New York City. These proposals would create a public wifi program, require the 311 service center to help residents find affordable internet options, establish a system for reviewing city-developed apps and electronic services, and provide information about affordable internet programs to students and families. All four bills were sent to the Committee on Technology for further review.

The Council also advanced several other measures, including a resolution urging state lawmakers to regulate the cryptocurrency industry better and protect investors, a resolution requiring schools to get parental permission before sharing student data with outside contractors, a bill to penalize the sale of unsafe electric bikes and batteries, a bill to track city revenue from fines and violations, and a bill to install panic buttons in houses of worship. These items were referred to their respective committees for consideration.

### Summary:
Council approved additional funding for the Denver Construction Careers Program, adding $300,000 to support job training for construction workers through the end of 2026. They also authorized the airport to purchase capital equipment like vehicles needed for airport operations.

In a zoning decision that drew public support, council voted to rezone a property at 4631 North Josephine Street in the Elyria Swansea neighborhood to allow for higher-density residential development. An upcoming hearing is scheduled for April 6 on a proposed zoning change for properties in the Gateway-Green Valley Ranch area.

### Summary:
The New York City Council approved two laws focused on regulating electric bicycles and powered mobility devices. The first law requires businesses selling or renting e-bikes and similar devices to post safety information about lithium-ion batteries. The second law increases penalties for illegal sales of these devices, requires online sellers to provide certification that products have been safety tested, and gives the Fire Department authority to enforce violations.

Both measures passed by unanimous consent. They address safety concerns around e-bikes and scooters, which have become more common in the city. The laws aim to reduce fires and injuries by ensuring devices meet safety standards and consumers have proper information about battery risks.

### Summary:
The city council scheduled several spending and service agreements for committee review in May and June 2026. These include: a $1.5 million electrical equipment contract with Graybar Electric Company for up to five years; a $2.65 million workforce development program to provide job training and employment services for adults and youth through 2027; a $1.5 million firefighter equipment purchase agreement with LN Curtis & Sons (potentially extending to $2.5 million over five years); and a partnership with USC's School of Social Work to create internship opportunities for graduate students in the police department from 2026 to 2031.

The council also scheduled informational reports on the Community Police Review Agency's activities and the Mayor's Commission on Persons with Disabilities' status. Additionally, the city will consider adopting a 2026-2031 Local Hazard Mitigation Plan to address emergency preparedness and safety risks, pending approval from state and federal agencies.

### Summary:
The city council approved several spending items including a $30 million, three-year contract with W.W. Clyde & Co. for construction work at Denver International Airport, and a $575,245 purchase order for replacement police vehicles from Asbury Automotive Group. The council also approved additional funding of $728,750 to Base Tactical Disaster Recovery for administering federal emergency funds, and $994,000 to Verizon for city cellular service through the end of 2026.

The city also approved a $4.5 million loan to support the construction and rehabilitation of 133 affordable housing units in Council District 10, and dedicated a city-owned parcel of land as part of the public right-of-way for North Federal Boulevard. Additionally, the council approved an $81,411 equipment upgrade for the fire department's 107-foot aerial fire truck.

### Summary:
The city's License and Consumer Protection Committee voted to recommend several changes to where liquor stores and package goods shops can open across Chicago. They approved new licenses on portions of South Ewing Avenue, South Halsted Street, South Wallace Street, and North Central Avenue, while blocking new licenses on West 111th Street and various other streets.

The committee also recommended a new rule for the 37th Precinct in the 13th Ward that would ban additional shared housing units and vacation rentals in certain residential areas. This is meant to restrict the growth of short-term rentals and multi-unit housing in those neighborhoods.

### Summary:
The Board of Supervisors approved changes to the Planning Code for a house at 657 Chenery Street and designated the 1100 block of Pierce Street between Turk and Eddy streets as "Carmen Johnson Way" to honor her decades of service to the Fillmore neighborhood. The board also confirmed Alison Warner's appointment to the Redevelopment Successor Agency Oversight Board, a position that oversees community investment and infrastructure decisions, with her term ending in January 2028.

The board presented certificates of honor to several individuals and organizations, including the Lord Mayor of Cork for the sister city partnership, Edgewood Center for Children and Families on their 175th anniversary, City College of San Francisco's Mission Center dean, the Castro Halloween Night Market organizers, and the Jing Mo Athletic Association. During public comment, residents raised concerns about property transfer taxes, neighborhood issues in the Western Addition and Fillmore, city technology contracts, street closures on Haight Street, and healthcare access.

### Summary:
Council approved several spending agreements across Denver city departments. The Parks and Culture Committee approved $400,000 in additional funding for material placement services in Denver parks (bringing the total contract to $1.2 million through 2028), $369,800 more for maintenance at Skyline Park through 2027, and modified the fee structure for a senior fitness program at recreation centers due to federal funding changes. Council also approved $300,000 in additional funding for cleaning and maintenance at the city's indoor firearms training facility through 2028, and a $1.89 million contract with Pinkard Construction to replace electrical equipment at Boettcher Concert Hall.

One resolution regarding parks materials placement was postponed to March 31, 2026 for further consideration. All other votes passed.

### Summary:
Council approved several service contracts and agreements. The city will use W.J. Dickensheet & Associates to auction off impound vehicles through 2031, allow Verizon to install cellular equipment at the City and County Building at no cost to the city, and spend $4.6 million on an AI-powered plan review system through 2031 to speed up the permitting process. The city also extended its contract with Versaterm Public Safety for police software systems, adding $472,110 to continue through 2030.

The council also approved funding for public health and food assistance programs. The Colorado Coalition for the Homeless received an additional $203,789 to extend its nutrition program for Denver kids through 2027, and the city accepted federal HIV emergency relief funding from the U.S. Department of Health and Human Services.

CITY: Denver DATE: 2026-04-14 BODY: Parks,_Art_and_Culture AGENDA ITEM: Consent Items AGENDA ITEM: Approves a grant agreement with Colorado Department of Public Health and Environment for $952,000.00 with an end date of 12-31-2029 for implementation of the Montbello Landscape Transformation Project, which will restore approximately 17 acres of non-native turf grass in Montbello to a native landscape, in Council District 11 (PARKS-202580159). ACTION: Council Resolution 26-0497 was approved by consent.

### Summary:
Denver's Parks, Art and Culture committee approved a $952,000 grant from the Colorado Department of Public Health and Environment to restore 17 acres of land in the Montbello neighborhood. The project will replace non-native grass with native plants and is scheduled to be completed by the end of 2029.

The grant was approved by consent vote on April 14, 2026. This project will affect residents in Council District 11, primarily improving the landscape and environmental quality of the Montbello area.

### Summary:
The committee discussed updates to Seattle's stormwater code to meet state requirements and strengthen how the city handles stormwater for development and maintenance projects. The committee also recommended the City Council pass two ordinances affecting Seattle Public Utilities: one to accept easements on two private properties on NE 91st Street for slope stabilization work, and another to acquire two parcels of land on South Director Street (either through negotiation or eminent domain if necessary).

The committee recommended confirming three appointments to the Seattle Ethics and Elections Commission: Evan M. Smith (through December 2027), Andrea Lino (through December 2028), and Anita Khandelwal (through December 2026). The committee also heard a presentation about a homemade food waste program from Homemade's CEO and leadership.

### Summary:
The council unanimously approved a contract with Cisco to replace the city's telephone system. The initial cost will be about $104,000 to purchase 450 new phones, with annual service fees of $95,000 per year. The city currently pays $104,100 annually plus additional maintenance costs, so the move should save money overall. The new system will let employees use computers with headsets instead of desk phones if needed, and the city will keep its current phone numbers. The transition is expected to happen within a month.

The council also voted unanimously to have the city attorney revise the Public Expression policy for public comments during council meetings. Councilman Gallagher raised concerns about requiring speakers to announce their address during public comment periods and asked for the policy to be reviewed and changed. Several council members reported attending various community events, including a school safety forum, a Convoy of Hope event, and park dedications.

### Summary:
The City Council passed several resolutions calling on the state to address housing, wages, and climate issues. These included requests to require higher wages for school aides across all school types, fund rent arrears owed by public housing residents since the pandemic, and invest $3 billion annually into the NYC Housing Authority to repair aging public housing buildings. The council also called for state action on a working families tax credit to help lower-income New Yorkers and passage of the NY HEAT Act to align utility regulation with climate goals.

The council also approved resolutions focused on local governance and safety. These included condemning human trafficking of migrants, requiring the Housing Authority to improve its contract database with better search features, mandating that public housing caretakers obtain safety training certifications, and supporting state legislation to include community projects in job creation programs.

### Summary:
Denver City Council approved seven contracts with design firms to handle landscape architecture, planning, and design work across the city. The firms and their contract amounts are: DHM Design Corporation ($2.5 million), EM Dub Design ($1.5 million), Flow Design Collaborative ($1.5 million), Goodbee & Associates ($1.5 million), Hord Coplan Macht ($2.5 million), Livable Cities Studios ($2.5 million), and Matrix Design Group ($3 million). Each contract runs for four years with an option to extend for one additional year.

These are "on-call" contracts, meaning the city can hire these firms as needed for parks and public space projects citywide. All contracts were approved by the full council on the same vote.

### Summary:
The Board of Supervisors assigned several proposals to committee for review. A new Fire Code update (effective January 1, 2023) will replace the existing code with the 2022 California Fire Code plus San Francisco amendments, affecting permit and inspection fees. An ordinance would allow nighttime entertainment venues as permitted uses on Folsom and 11th Streets in SoMa to support the leather and LGBTQ cultural district. Another proposal would exclude historic districts from the HOME-SF affordable housing program. A holiday season measure would waive street vending permit fees for sidewalk sales on three weekends in November and December.

The District Attorney's office was authorized to accept two insurance fraud prevention grants totaling over $1.3 million from the state for the fiscal year starting July 1, 2022. Additionally, the Board received notice of a lawsuit challenging the federal government's decision to cut funding to Laguna Honda Hospital without ensuring safe patient transfers or allowing the city's appeal to be heard first.

### Summary:
The city approved three emergency demolition contracts totaling about $60,738 for unsafe buildings at five addresses (5357 Iroquois, 3892 Bewick, 7130 Webb, and 544 Jefferson). The council also approved a $5.1 million contract for a fire department aerial ladder truck. All of these are fully funded by the city.

In other actions, the council deferred demolition orders on three properties (11534 Nottingham, 16700 Asbury, and 17694 Fenton) after inspections found the buildings could be repaired rather than torn down. The council also approved several street encroachment requests for outdoor seating and a canopy. Additionally, the city accepted increased grant funding: $12,040 more for an auto theft prevention program in Grosse Pointe and $200,000 more for a childhood lead poisoning prevention program.

### Summary:
The city council approved $20.2 million for window and door replacement projects at Boston public schools, including Adams Elementary School and Gardens School. The city will seek grant funding from the Massachusetts School Building Authority to help cover these costs. The money will be managed by the Public Facilities Department on behalf of Boston Public Schools.

The council also adopted a resolution naming March 18, 2026, "Boston Groundwater Trust Day" to recognize the 40th anniversary of the Boston Groundwater Trust. Additionally, a home rule petition regarding voting rights for all legal residents in Boston was referred to the Committee on Government Operations for further review.

### Summary:
Denver's Health and Safety Committee approved a $3.5 million contract with Michael's of Denver Catering to provide meals and snacks to children 18 and under through the city's Tasty Food Program. The contract runs through the end of 2031 and covers program sites across the city.

The committee postponed a vote on a separate $150,000 contract with Axon Enterprise for an automatic license plate recognition system with 50 cameras to be installed citywide. The vote was pushed to March 31, 2026, after Council member Flynn requested a delay at the March 23 meeting.

### Summary:
The city approved several technology and service contracts: $1.8 million annually for Amazon Web Services software citywide, $800,000 additional funding for records storage and destruction services, and nearly $300,000 to extend a digital media management platform contract through 2029. The city also extended a design contract with Merrick & Company for one year to continue infrastructure work at the National Western Center campus in Council District 9.

Council also approved a 2026 collective bargaining agreement with the Denver Sheriff's police union and moved forward a zoning code change that would extend how long site development plans remain valid. A public hearing on the zoning change is scheduled for May 4, 2026.

### Summary:
The city is selling six vacant or underused properties to private buyers at low prices to encourage development and business activity. The properties include: a contractor yard ($850,000), an office space for a 3-D printing business ($2,701), parking lot ($7,256), a home office for a landscaping business ($10,500), equipment storage for a restaurant/gas station ($25,000), and customer parking ($5,010). All proposed uses match the zoning for their locations, and one buyer must secure the property within six months and obtain occupancy permits within two years or the city can take it back.

These property sales were referred to the Planning and Economic Development Committee in January 2017. The deals aim to bring vacant buildings back into use and attract small businesses to Detroit neighborhoods.

### Summary:
The committee recommended approval for 35 businesses and organizations to bypass Chicago's standard physical barrier requirements for their commercial alley driveways. These exemptions affect auto repair shops, tire shops, early learning centers, funeral homes, and various other businesses across the city—allowing them to access alleys without the typical safety barriers that are normally required.

The committee also approved five honorary street designations honoring community members (including Reverend Dr. Leon D. Finney Jr., Terry Miller, Carmen V. Gaspar, Lola Navarro, James W. Cole, and Don Selle), authorized opening two public streets (one between W. 38th and May/Morgan Streets, another near Division Street), approved a property resubdivision at McCormick Square, and vacated an alley and dedicated a public way in the South Homan Avenue area.

### Summary:
The committee approved settlement of a lawsuit against the city for $15,000 related to a bus incident, and extended a custodial services contract with Kristel Group Inc. through June 2017 with an additional $1.36 million in funding.

The committee also referred several new contracts for approval, including: $250,000 for tree and shrub planting; three separate park renovation projects totaling about $3.4 million; $80,400 for riding mowers; cleanup and debris removal from vacant lots for about $16.7 million over three years; and smaller contracts for a poll worker trainer ($30,000), law clerk ($37,100), and prisoner reentry services ($212,446).
